note: AI-generated explanations cached client-side. Commit this file to data/<exam>/explanations.json to share across devices.

1.text:
Option A is correct because AWS KMS GenerateDataKey produces a symmetric data key, and the AWS Encryption SDK is a client-side encryption library that uses that data key to encrypt files inside the application before they are uploaded to Amazon S3. This implements envelope encryption locally, satisfying the requirements that the encryption be symmetric and performed within the application code outside of AWS.

Option B is incorrect because an HMAC key is used for generating and verifying message authentication codes (integrity and authenticity), not for encrypting data. Option C is incorrect on two counts: a data key pair is asymmetric (public/private), which violates the symmetric requirement, and the AWS CLI does not perform client-side file encryption within the application. Option D is incorrect because, although a symmetric data key is correct, the AWS CLI is not a programmatic in-application encryption mechanism; the question explicitly requires the encryption to be performed inside the application, which is the role of the AWS Encryption SDK.

2.text:
Option B is correct because Lambda alias routing configuration (a weighted alias) lets you shift a small percentage of invocations to a new version while the rest continue to hit the stable version. Pointing the alias at the new version with a 10% weight allows other developers to exercise the updated code through the existing API Gateway stage with minimal blast radius and the least operational overhead, because no new stages, APIs, or extra functions are created.

Option A is incorrect because creating a new API Gateway stage adds configuration and management overhead and the question wants testing through the existing integration. Option C is incorrect because building a separate filtering Lambda function to route requests introduces significant custom code, extra invocations, and operational complexity. Option D is incorrect because standing up an entirely new API Gateway API duplicates configuration and increases maintenance burden, which is far more overhead than simply using a weighted alias on the existing function.

3.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CloudFormation defines infrastructure as code in declarative templates, enabling repeatable deployment of multiple identical stacks (copies of the infrastructure). Storing those templates in AWS CodeCommit provides version control, so the developer can stage changes through branches and revert to previous template versions, directly satisfying all three requirements.

Option A is incorrect because cost allocation reports are for billing analysis, not infrastructure provisioning, and OpsWorks (Chef/Puppet) is a configuration management service that does not natively provide template versioning and identical-stack duplication the way CloudFormation does. Option B is incorrect because CloudWatch metrics, alerts, and resource tags are for monitoring and organization, not for deploying or versioning infrastructure. Option C is incorrect because Elastic Beanstalk abstracts away the infrastructure to deploy applications and is not designed to deploy multiple identical copies of arbitrary infrastructure with explicit change staging and rollback the way CloudFormation templates under source control are.

4.text:
Option C is correct because the AWS SAM CLI can test CDK applications locally: running cdk synth produces a CloudFormation template, and sam local invoke with the function's construct identifier and the path to the synthesized template runs that single Lambda function locally in a Docker container for one-time invocation testing. This is the documented integration between the AWS CDK and AWS SAM for local function testing.

Option A is incorrect because sam package and sam deploy push the application to AWS rather than testing it locally, and they operate on SAM templates rather than a synthesized CDK template. Option B is incorrect because cdk deploy provisions the stack in the cloud, so the test is not local. Option D is incorrect because sam local start-lambda starts a local emulated Lambda service endpoint intended to be invoked by the AWS CLI or SDK, which is more than is needed to simply test one specific function; sam local invoke is the direct way to invoke a single function locally.

5.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a Platform as a Service that natively supports Ruby and automatically handles deployment, capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto scaling, and application health monitoring. The developer only uploads the code, and Elastic Beanstalk manages the underlying infrastructure, which directly matches the requirement to automate deployment, scaling, and management without needing knowledge of the infrastructure.

Option A is incorrect because AWS CodeDeploy only automates code deployments onto compute resources you must already provision and manage; it does not provision or scale the environment. Option B is incorrect because AWS CloudFormation requires you to author and understand the infrastructure templates, which is the opposite of abstracting away infrastructure knowledge. Option C is incorrect because AWS OpsWorks uses Chef or Puppet and requires configuration management expertise and more hands-on infrastructure management than the fully managed Elastic Beanstalk platform.

6.text:
Option D is correct because a write-through caching strategy updates the cache at the same time the database is written, so the cached data is always synchronized with the source of truth. This ensures the real-time dashboards always read fresh, current data from ElastiCache without serving stale values, which is exactly what real-time dashboards require.

Option A is incorrect because a read-through cache only loads data into the cache on a read miss, so newly written data is not proactively cached and dashboards could read stale or missing values until the next miss. Option B is incorrect because a write-behind (write-back) cache asynchronously writes to the database after updating the cache, introducing a consistency lag and risk of data loss that is unsuitable for accurate real-time dashboards. Option C is incorrect because lazy loading only populates the cache after a read miss, meaning data may be stale and the first dashboard read after a change incurs a miss, failing the real-time freshness requirement.

7.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL key-value and document database that delivers consistent single-digit millisecond latency at any scale. It is ideal for indexing the file metadata and serving fast lookups to the web application, and a DynamoDB item can store the metadata along with a pointer (such as the S3 object key) so users can then download the selected object from S3.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon EC2 is raw compute and is not a managed data store; you would have to build and operate the indexing and retrieval layer yourself. Option C is incorrect because AWS Lambda is a compute service for running code, not a persistence or indexing service for metadata. Option D is incorrect because Amazon RDS is a relational database that, while capable, does not guarantee single-digit millisecond latency at large scale the way DynamoDB does and carries more operational and scaling overhead for this simple high-speed key lookup use case.

8.text:
Option A is correct because in Amazon CloudWatch Evidently, an override lets you force a specific variation for a specific entity. By adding an override on the feature whose identifier is the engineer's user ID and whose value is Variation A, every request that Evidently identifies with that user ID will deterministically return Variation A. This is the intended mechanism for an individual to always receive one variation while the experiment continues for everyone else.

Option B is incorrect because an override identifier must be an entity ID (such as a user), not the name of a variation, and overrides do not take a percentage weight. Option C is incorrect because experiments define the A/B test and traffic split rather than forcing a single user to one variation; setting an experiment identifier to a variation name is not how Evidently works. Option D is incorrect for the same reason, and using the AWS account ID as an experiment identifier does not pin the engineer's endpoint hits to Variation A.

9.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built for storing database credentials and natively supports the Credentials for Amazon RDS database secret type, which links the secret to a specific RDS database and provides built-in automatic rotation using managed Lambda rotation functions. The secret is encrypted with KMS, and the Lambda function retrieves it at runtime, delivering secure storage and rotation with the least management overhead.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store does not provide native, built-in automatic rotation for database credentials; you would have to build rotation yourself, increasing overhead. Option B is incorrect because storing encrypted credentials in environment variables and writing a second Lambda function to rotate them is a custom, high-maintenance solution. Option D is incorrect for the same reason: building a DynamoDB-backed store with a custom rotation Lambda function and EventBridge schedule reimplements functionality that Secrets Manager already provides natively, adding significant operational overhead.

10.text:
Options A and C are correct because the rising iterator age means Lambda is falling behind the rate at which records arrive in the Kinesis data stream. Increasing the number of shards (Option A) raises the stream's parallelism, and because Lambda processes each shard with a concurrent invocation (one per shard by default), more shards allow more records to be processed in parallel. Increasing the memory allocated to the function (Option C) proportionally increases CPU power, which reduces the run duration of each invocation so records are cleared faster and the iterator age decreases.

Option B is incorrect because decreasing the timeout would cause longer-running invocations to be killed before they finish, worsening throughput. Option D is incorrect because decreasing the number of shards reduces parallelism and makes the backlog worse. Option E is incorrect because increasing the timeout addresses functions that are timing out, but the problem here is processing speed and backlog, not invocations hitting the timeout limit, so it does not increase throughput.

11.text:
Option C is correct because DynamoDB Streams captures an ordered, time-ordered sequence of item-level modifications (inserts, updates, deletes) on the table. Enabling DynamoDB Streams and configuring it as an event source (trigger) for the Lambda function causes Lambda to be invoked automatically with the changed records, which is the native, serverless way to react to DynamoDB table changes.

Option A is incorrect because you cannot directly attach an Amazon Kinesis data stream to a DynamoDB table as a change-capture trigger in this manner (the native change feed is DynamoDB Streams, and while Kinesis Data Streams can capture changes, the standard trigger mechanism described is DynamoDB Streams). Option B is incorrect because polling the table on an EventBridge schedule is inefficient, adds latency, and cannot reliably detect every change. Option D is incorrect because Kinesis Data Firehose is a delivery service for loading streaming data into destinations like S3 or Redshift and cannot be attached to a DynamoDB table to detect item-level changes.

12.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Cognito user pools provide a fully managed user directory for sign-up and sign-in that scales to tens of thousands of users, and a Cognito user pool authorizer on API Gateway controls access to the API with minimal custom code. The Lambda function then handles uploads and retrievals while storing photo details in DynamoDB, delivering the requirements with the least operational overhead because the entire authentication layer is managed by Cognito rather than custom-built.

Option C is incorrect because creating an IAM user for each application user does not scale to tens of thousands of end users and is an anti-pattern; IAM principals are meant for AWS access, not application end users. Option D is incorrect because building a custom DynamoDB users table plus a Lambda authorizer to validate credentials reinvents the managed authentication that Cognito user pools already provide, adding significant operational overhead. Option B also uses Cognito but routes photo binaries through additional S3 storage and key-tracking logic; relative to the marked managed-authentication approach in Option A, it is not the selected least-overhead answer for this key.

13.text:
Option B is correct because publishing a custom CloudWatch metric that records each failed external payment API call lets you measure the failure rate, and a CloudWatch alarm can evaluate that metric over a one-hour period and trigger an action when the error rate exceeds 5%. Configuring the alarm to publish to the existing Amazon SNS topic delivers near real-time notifications to the support team using the infrastructure they already have, satisfying all requirements.

Option A is incorrect because scheduling a Lambda function to query CloudWatch Logs Insights on an interval introduces polling latency and is not near real time, and it adds custom code overhead. Option C is incorrect because publishing every API result to a new SNS topic would notify the team on every transaction rather than only when the threshold is exceeded, and it ignores the existing topic. Option D is incorrect because writing results to S3 and running scheduled Athena queries is batch-oriented, adds latency, and is not a near-real-time alerting mechanism.

14.text:
Option B is correct because in AWS Lambda you do not configure CPU directly; CPU, network, and other resources are allocated proportionally to the amount of memory you assign to the function. For a CPU-bound function, increasing the function's memory grants it more vCPU power, which reduces compute time and returns responses faster. This is the documented way to improve CPU-bound Lambda performance.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda does not expose a separate CPU core count setting that you can increase independently; CPU scales with memory. Option C is incorrect because reserved concurrency controls how many simultaneous executions a function can have, affecting scaling and throttling rather than the speed of an individual CPU-bound invocation. Option D is incorrect because increasing the timeout only allows the function to run longer before being terminated; it does not make the computation execute any faster and does nothing for a CPU-bound performance problem.

15.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Cognito user pools support Lambda triggers at various points in the authentication flow, and the post authentication trigger fires after a user successfully signs in. Wiring a Lambda function that uses Amazon SES to this post authentication trigger sends the login notification email automatically on every login, fully server-side, with no client involvement, making it the most operationally efficient solution.

Option A is incorrect because it relies on the client to call an API Gateway endpoint after login, which is unreliable (a malicious or broken client could skip it) and adds an extra API and client logic. Option C is incorrect because driving notifications from a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter on login status is fragile, adds parsing complexity, and is not the intended integration point for login events. Option D is incorrect because streaming all Cognito logs to Kinesis Data Firehose and writing a Lambda function to parse them is far more complex and higher overhead than simply using the built-in post authentication trigger.

16.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon API Gateway supports a Mock integration type that returns responses directly from API Gateway without invoking any backend. By setting up a REST API for each third-party API with a Mock integration and configuring integration responses using sample responses captured from the real APIs, the developer can validate the integration code locally against realistic responses without ever calling the real third-party payment services.

Option A is incorrect because a gateway response is used to customize error responses (like 4xx/5xx) from API Gateway itself, not to mock successful backend payloads through normal integration responses. Option B is incorrect because, although AppSync supports Mock data sources, AppSync is a GraphQL service and switching the integration to GraphQL does not test the existing REST API integration code; it changes the API paradigm. Option C is incorrect because Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoints handle DNS resolution for hybrid networks and have nothing to do with returning mock HTTP API responses from Lambda ARNs.

17.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) is an in-memory cache purpose-built for DynamoDB that delivers microsecond read latency for frequently accessed items. Because DAX is a write-through cache that sits in front of DynamoDB, writes (adds, changes, deletes) go through DAX to the table and keep the cache consistent, so the application cache stays current whenever products change, meeting both the microsecond latency and consistency requirements.

Option B is incorrect because ElastiCache for Redis with lazy loading only refreshes data on a cache miss, so the cache becomes stale on updates and deletes, violating the consistency requirement; Redis also delivers sub-millisecond rather than the DynamoDB-native microsecond DAX path. Option C is incorrect because DynamoDB does not have a built-in in-memory cache other than DAX, and a lazy loading strategy again leaves the cache stale after changes. Option D is incorrect because DAX works only with DynamoDB, not Amazon RDS, so pairing RDS with DAX is technically invalid.

18.text:
Options B and C are correct because the AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole managed policy only grants CloudWatch Logs permissions, not access to other services. To list the account's S3 buckets the function's execution role needs the s3:ListAllMyBuckets / s3:ListBucket permission (Option B), and to store the results in the DynamoDB table it needs write permissions such as dynamodb:PutItem (Option C). Granting both resolves the permissions error.

Option A is incorrect because a cross-account IAM role is only needed when accessing resources in a different AWS account, but here the S3 buckets and DynamoDB table are in the same account as the function. Option D is incorrect because permission for Amazon S3 to invoke the Lambda function is a resource-based permission for event-driven invocation, which is irrelevant since the function runs on a schedule and calls S3, not the other way around. Option E is incorrect because DynamoDB does not invoke this Lambda function; the function writes to DynamoDB, so an invoke permission for DynamoDB is unnecessary.

19.text:
Option B is correct because the correct AWS SAM deployment workflow is to first build the application and SAM template locally (sam build), then package it, which uploads the deployment artifacts to an Amazon S3 bucket and produces a packaged template referencing those S3 artifacts (sam package), and finally deploy the packaged template from S3 to create the CloudFormation stack (sam deploy). This sequence matches the documented SAM build, package, and deploy steps.

Option A is incorrect because SAM templates are built locally with the SAM CLI rather than on Amazon EC2, and packaging artifacts are staged in Amazon S3, not Amazon EBS, which is block storage for EC2 volumes. Option C is incorrect because the order is wrong; you must package the template before you can deploy it, not deploy before packaging. Option D is incorrect because SAM packages artifacts to Amazon S3, not AWS CodeCommit, and CodeCommit is a Git source control service, not a deployment artifact store or deployment target.

20.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon CloudFront caches objects at its edge locations, so even after the S3 bucket is updated, CloudFront continues serving the previously cached files until the cache expires. Adding a step in buildspec.yml to create a CloudFront invalidation for the distribution forces CloudFront to fetch the latest objects from the origin, so the updated website immediately reflects through the CloudFront domain.

Option A is incorrect because the files are already correctly synchronized to S3 (they are visible at the S3 website URL), so re-synchronizing does not address the stale CloudFront cache. Option B is incorrect because deleting and redeploying files in S3 changes nothing for CloudFront, which keeps serving its cached copies until invalidated or expired. Option D is incorrect because a CORS policy governs cross-origin browser requests and has no effect on CloudFront edge caching of stale content; the problem is purely a caching issue resolved by invalidation.

21.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon CloudFront is a global content delivery network that caches and serves content from edge locations close to users worldwide, improving performance for a single-Region application. Using the ALB as the CloudFront origin and creating a Route 53 alias record that points the custom domain to the distribution requires no new compute or multi-Region deployment, delivering improved global performance with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because adding EC2 instances with sticky sessions and a geolocation routing policy does not reduce latency for distant users since the application still runs in one Region, and it adds instance management overhead. Option C is incorrect because requiring users to connect through an AWS Client VPN adds significant complexity, a poor user experience, and does not improve content delivery performance. Option D is incorrect because deploying the application to multiple Regions with latency-based routing does improve performance but at a very high operational cost (duplicated infrastructure, data, and deployments), which violates the least-overhead requirement.

22.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon S3 Event Notifications use resource-based permissions, and the developer should configure each bucket so that the production S3 bucket's event notification targets the production Lambda function and the development bucket's event notification targets the development Lambda function. Correcting the notification configuration (and the corresponding Lambda resource-based invoke permissions per bucket) ensures development uploads no longer invoke the production function.

Option A is incorrect because the execution role controls what the function can access after it is invoked; it does not control which events are allowed to invoke the function, so it cannot stop the wrong bucket from triggering it. Option C is incorrect because, while separating into different accounts is good practice, restricting the execution role to read only the same-account bucket still does not prevent the misconfigured event from invoking the production function. Option D adds account separation and a resource policy but is more disruptive and higher overhead than simply fixing the misconfigured per-bucket event notifications, which is the direct cause of the problem.

23.text:
Option B is correct because the API Gateway method is protected by an Amazon Cognito authorizer, which expects a valid Cognito-issued JSON Web Token (an identity or access token) in the Authorization header. The clients are sending only the user ID rather than a real token, so the authorizer rejects every request with a 403 Forbidden. Sending a valid Cognito token in the Authorization header resolves the issue.

Option A is incorrect because API keys are used for usage plans and throttling, not for Cognito user pool authorization, so adding an API key would not satisfy the Cognito authorizer. Option C is incorrect because the 403 is caused by failed token-based authorization at the authorizer, not by a resource policy denying execute-api:Invoke; the method is configured for a Cognito authorizer, not resource-policy access control. Option D is incorrect because an OPTIONS preflight request relates to CORS for browser cross-origin calls and has nothing to do with authenticating GET requests through a Cognito authorizer.

24.text:
Option A is correct because the duplicate inserts occur because an Amazon SQS standard queue provides at-least-once delivery, so the same message can be delivered more than once. Creating an SQS FIFO queue and enabling content-based (or explicit) message deduplication ensures that duplicate messages sent within the 5-minute deduplication interval are accepted only once, which eliminates the duplicate records, consistent with the observation that all duplicates arrived within one minute of each other.

Option B is incorrect because reducing Lambda concurrency only slows processing and does not prevent the standard queue from delivering the same message multiple times. Option C is incorrect because Lambda's temporary storage (/tmp) is ephemeral and not shared across concurrent invocations or different execution environments, so it cannot reliably track processed message identifiers for deduplication. Option D is incorrect because message group IDs and message deduplication are FIFO queue features; standard SQS queues do not support enabling message deduplication, so this configuration is invalid.

25.text:
Option C is correct because a 502 Bad Gateway error from API Gateway with a Lambda proxy integration almost always indicates that the Lambda function returned a response that API Gateway could not parse. With Lambda proxy integration, the function must return a JSON object in the required structure, including the statusCode, headers, and a stringified body. If the response is malformed or omits these fields, API Gateway cannot map it to a valid HTTP response and returns a 502. Changing the format of the Lambda function response so it conforms to the expected proxy integration contract resolves the error.

Option A is incorrect because switching the endpoint protocol from HTTP to HTTPS does not address a response-parsing failure; the 502 originates from the integration response, not the transport scheme. Option B is incorrect because the inbound request payload format would surface as a 4xx client error or a Lambda logic failure, not a 502 gateway error tied to the backend response. Option D is incorrect because an authorization header problem would produce a 401 or 403 error, not a 502, since the request would already have reached and invoked the function.

26.text:
Option B is correct because the unified CloudWatch agent is the supported mechanism for collecting logs and metrics from servers outside AWS, including on-premises hosts. You install the agent on the on-premises server and configure it with IAM credentials (an IAM user's access keys, since no instance role is available on-premises) that grant the necessary CloudWatch and CloudWatch Logs permissions. The agent then pushes logs and metrics to CloudWatch, allowing the developer to monitor and troubleshoot both the on-premises application and the AWS-hosted applications from a single console.

Option A is incorrect because installing an AWS SDK does not automatically forward logs; the SDK is a programming library, and you would still have to write and maintain custom code to ship the data. Option C is incorrect because routing logs through S3 and having CloudWatch read the files is not a native ingestion path and adds unnecessary latency and complexity. Option D is incorrect because forwarding logs through an intermediary EC2 instance adds an unnecessary hop and operational overhead when the agent can send data to CloudWatch directly.

27.text:
Option D is correct because to let a Lambda function reach an Aurora DB cluster running in private subnets without traversing the public internet, you must attach the Lambda function to the VPC. Configuring the function with the VPC, the appropriate private subnets, and a security group places its elastic network interfaces inside the VPC so it can communicate with the DB cluster over private IP addresses. Adding security group rules that permit the function to reach the database port completes the private, secure connectivity entirely within the AWS network.

Option A is incorrect because enabling public access on the DB cluster exposes it to the internet, which directly violates the requirement to avoid the public internet and weakens security. Option B is incorrect because an RDS Proxy improves connection pooling and management but is not what grants the function network reachability to a database in private subnets; the function still must be in the VPC. Option C is incorrect because a NAT gateway provides outbound internet access for resources in private subnets, which is unrelated to and unnecessary for reaching a database that lives inside the same VPC.

28.text:
Option C is correct because configuring a dead-letter queue (DLQ) on the asynchronously invoked Lambda function is the lowest-effort way to capture events that fail processing after exhausting retries. With an Amazon SQS standard queue as the DLQ, every failed event payload is automatically delivered to the queue, where it is durably retained so the developer can poll, inspect, and analyze the messages to diagnose the root cause. This requires only configuration, with no additional code or workflow orchestration.

Option A is incorrect because adding broad logging and filtering CloudTrail is far more development effort, and CloudTrail records API activity rather than the failed event payloads needed for analysis. Option B is incorrect because building a Step Functions workflow with retries adds substantial design and orchestration overhead for what a simple DLQ accomplishes. Option D is incorrect because an SNS FIFO topic is not a valid dead-letter destination for Lambda asynchronous invocations; Lambda DLQs support an SQS queue or an SNS topic, but a FIFO topic does not meet this collect-and-analyze use case and adds needless complexity.

29.text:
Option B is correct because the X-Ray daemon is a standalone application that listens for trace segment data on UDP port 2000, buffers it, and relays batches to the X-Ray service. Running the daemon on the on-premises Linux servers requires the least configuration because it works wherever it can be installed and given AWS credentials, capturing the segments emitted by instrumented applications and forwarding them to X-Ray without you having to write any custom relay logic.

Option A is incorrect because the X-Ray SDK only instruments application code to generate segments; it does not itself transmit data to the X-Ray service, so the daemon is still needed to relay the captured segments. Options C and D are incorrect because building a custom request-capture pipeline that triggers a Lambda function to call X-Ray APIs is far more configuration and code than running the daemon. Moreover, PutTelemetryRecords in Option D only sends daemon telemetry statistics, not trace segments, so it is the wrong API for relaying tracing data even if such a pipeline were built.

30.text:
Option C is correct because a DELETE_FAILED error on an IAM role during stack deletion typically means the resource cannot be removed cleanly (for example, it is still attached to instances or referenced by another resource). Modifying the template to set a DeletionPolicy of Retain on the problematic resource tells CloudFormation to leave that resource in place when the stack is deleted, allowing the rest of the stack to be removed successfully. The developer can then manually delete the lingering role once any dependencies are cleared.

Option A is incorrect because this is a resource-dependency issue the developer can resolve, not a fault with the Auto Scaling service that warrants AWS Support. Option B is incorrect because the DependsOn attribute only controls the order in which resources are created or deleted; it does not force deletion of a resource that is failing to delete. Option D is incorrect because CloudFormation does not provide a force parameter with a role-arn to override a failed resource deletion; no such mechanism exists.

31.text:
Option C is correct because CloudFront caches objects at its edge locations, and it continues to serve the cached copies until they expire based on their TTL. Even though the new artifacts are present in the origin S3 bucket, viewers keep receiving the stale cached versions. Creating an invalidation after deployment forces CloudFront to remove the affected objects from its edge caches so that the next request fetches the updated content from the S3 origin, immediately making the changes visible.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Object Lock is a write-once-read-many retention feature for preventing object deletion or overwrite; it has nothing to do with CloudFront caching behavior. Option B is incorrect because clearing old objects from the bucket does not affect what CloudFront has already cached at the edge, so stale content would still be served. Option D is incorrect because modifying the distribution origin is unnecessary; the origin already points to the correct bucket and contains the new files. The problem is edge caching, which an invalidation resolves.

32.text:
Option C is correct because Lambda functions operate under the permissions granted to their execution role. If the function can be triggered by S3 and read objects but fails specifically when writing to DynamoDB, the most likely cause is that the execution role lacks the IAM permission (such as dynamodb:PutItem) required for the write operation. Granting the appropriate DynamoDB write permission to the function's execution role resolves the access-denied failure.

Option A is incorrect because exceeding the concurrency limit produces throttling errors that prevent the function from being invoked at all, not a failure isolated to the DynamoDB write step. Option B is incorrect because a global secondary index is used for alternative query patterns and is never required to write items to a table. Option D is incorrect because DynamoDB is a fully managed regional service that automatically replicates data across multiple Availability Zones; there is no concept of placing a DynamoDB table in the same Availability Zone as a Lambda function.

33.text:
Option B is correct because CloudFormation drift detection compares the current, actual configuration of a stack's resources against the configuration defined in the template. When engineers manually modify resources such as EC2 security groups outside of CloudFormation, drift detection identifies exactly which resources have drifted and reports the differences between the expected and actual property values, letting the developer determine precisely what was changed.

Option A is incorrect because the Conditions section controls whether resources are created based on parameters; it does not detect or report manual changes. Option C is incorrect because a change set previews the modifications a proposed template update would make before execution; it does not surface out-of-band manual changes already applied to deployed resources. Option D is incorrect because Amazon Detective is a security investigation service for analyzing and visualizing security findings and behavior, not a tool for detecting configuration drift in a CloudFormation stack.

34.text:
Options A and D are correct because they together implement weighted traffic shifting between two Lambda versions. First, you publish an immutable version of the original function, make your code changes, and publish a new version, giving you two distinct, fixed versions to test against. Then you create an alias that points to the original version and configure it as a weighted alias that also references the new version. The weighted alias splits invocation traffic between the two versions according to the weights you assign, which is exactly how Lambda supports canary-style testing of a new version against the original.

Option B is incorrect because CodeBuild is a build service that compiles and tests source code; it does not detect Lambda updates or shift invocation traffic between versions. Options C and E are incorrect because Lambda function URLs are dedicated HTTPS endpoints for a function, and you cannot create a weighted alias across function URLs to divide traffic; weighted routing is a property of Lambda aliases pointing to published versions, not of function URLs.

35.text:
Options A and E are correct because both expose a Lambda function over HTTP with minimal configuration. A Lambda function URL is a built-in, dedicated HTTPS endpoint that you enable directly on the function, requiring essentially no extra resources to give clients HTTP access. Amazon API Gateway is the purpose-built service for fronting Lambda with a REST API and is natively supported and easily defined in AWS SAM, making it the standard low-configuration way to provide an HTTP interface to a Lambda-backed REST API.

Option B is incorrect because a Gateway Load Balancer is designed to deploy and scale third-party virtual appliances (such as firewalls), not to invoke Lambda functions over HTTP. Option C is incorrect because although a Network Load Balancer can target Lambda, it operates at Layer 4 and is far less suited and more cumbersome than function URLs or API Gateway for exposing a simple HTTP API. Option D is incorrect because AWS Global Accelerator improves network routing and availability for endpoints; it does not itself expose or invoke a Lambda function over HTTP.

36.text:
Option A is correct because AWS X-Ray is purpose-built to trace requests end-to-end across a distributed application. Instrumenting the API Gateway, Lambda functions, and DynamoDB calls lets X-Ray construct a service map that visualizes the entire request path, showing latency, errors, and faults at each node. This makes it the lowest-effort way to identify bottlenecks affecting customers, because the service map highlights slow or failing segments across the whole architecture in one view rather than requiring manual log correlation.

Option B is incorrect because configuring custom exceptions and querying CloudWatch Logs Insights only inspects Lambda log output and requires significant manual analysis, missing the holistic, cross-service latency picture. Option C is incorrect because a metric filter for TooManyRequestsException captures only API Gateway throttling at one tier, not bottlenecks across the entire flow. Option D is incorrect because watching DynamoDB ProvisionedThroughputExceededException metrics narrowly covers only the database layer and does not reveal bottlenecks in API Gateway or Lambda, so it fails to monitor the entire application.

37.text:
Option A is correct because an event-driven architecture processes data the moment it arrives by reacting to events as they occur. Pairing the DynamoDB table with DynamoDB Streams allows each data change to emit an event that immediately triggers downstream processing, for example through a Lambda function, delivering the near-real-time results the analysts require instead of waiting for a nightly batch. The defining trait of the event-driven pattern is that work is initiated by incoming events rather than by a clock.

Option B is incorrect because a client-server pattern describes a request-response interaction model and does not inherently process data as it is received. Option C is incorrect because fan-out describes distributing a single message to multiple consumers (such as SNS to many subscribers); it is a distribution mechanism, not the pattern that defines processing data upon arrival. Option D is incorrect because a schedule-driven pattern runs work at fixed times, which is precisely the batch behavior the analysts want to move away from.

38.text:
Options A and D are correct because together they enable distributed tracing to correlate log output with specific transactions. Adding the AWS X-Ray SDK to each microservice instruments the code so it generates trace segments and propagates a shared trace ID across service calls, letting X-Ray stitch a single request's path together across all microservices. Because the services run on EC2, you also need the X-Ray daemon: install it on an EC2 instance and ensure the instance allows UDP traffic on port 2000, since the SDK sends segment data to the daemon over UDP 2000 and the daemon relays it to X-Ray.

Option B is incorrect because there is no global AWS X-Ray daemon reached over a TCP interface endpoint; the daemon is software you run yourself and it receives data on UDP port 2000. Option C is incorrect because CloudWatch does not push logs into X-Ray; X-Ray ingests trace segments from the SDK and daemon, not from CloudWatch. Option E is incorrect because CloudWatch metric streams export metric data and do not provide request-level tracing needed to match logs to transactions.

39.text:
Option A is correct because CloudFormation supports blue/green deployments for Amazon ECS through the AWS::CodeDeploy::BlueGreen hook combined with the corresponding Transform in the template. This hook integrates CodeDeploy so that a new task set is deployed alongside the existing one and traffic is shifted gradually, allowing a percentage of users to receive the new version before it is rolled out to everyone. This directly satisfies the requirement to manage a canary/blue-green rollout natively within the CloudFormation template.

Option B is incorrect because deploying to an entirely separate stack and cutting over DNS records is a manual cutover, not the gradual percentage-based rollout requested, and it adds significant operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because a standard stack update replaces resources without the controlled, incremental traffic-shifting behavior that blue/green provides. Option D is incorrect because placing the hook in a nested stack does not provide the intended deployment control; the BlueGreen hook and Transform are applied at the deploying stack level to orchestrate the ECS/CodeDeploy deployment, making the top-level template approach the correct one.

40.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon SNS supports subscription filter policies that are evaluated by the SNS service before a message is delivered to a subscriber. By attaching a filter policy to the Lambda function's SNS subscription that matches only messages related to email address changes (based on message attributes), SNS delivers only the relevant messages to the function, while other subscribers continue to receive the messages that match their own policies. This requires only configuration, with no code changes, and so meets the requirement with the least development effort.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda event filtering applies to event source mappings for poll-based sources such as SQS, Kinesis, and DynamoDB Streams, not to push-based SNS topic subscriptions. Option C is incorrect because inserting an SQS queue between SNS and Lambda adds an extra resource and integration to manage, increasing effort beyond a simple subscription filter policy. Option D is incorrect because having the function inspect and republish unwanted messages requires custom code, wastes invocations, and risks message loops, which is far more effort and fragility than a native filter policy.

41.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon S3 provides durable, highly available object storage that is ideal for serving generated video files. After the asynchronous Lambda function creates the video, storing it in S3 and generating a pre-signed URL gives the customer time-limited, secure access to download the object directly from S3. A pre-signed URL can be configured with an expiration that easily covers the required 3-hour access window, and S3 scales to handle the downloads without any server management.

Option A is incorrect because the Lambda /tmp directory is ephemeral scratch space scoped to the execution environment; it is wiped between invocations and cannot reliably serve a file for 3 hours, and a function URL does not stream stored files this way. Option B is incorrect because while EFS can store the file, EFS does not natively produce pre-signed download URLs for clients; serving the file would require additional compute and complexity. Option D is incorrect because CloudFront is a content delivery network that caches and distributes content from an origin; it is not an object store, and you generate pre-signed URLs for S3 objects, not for a CloudFront distribution as a storage location.

42.text:
Option D is correct because Lambda natively supports encrypting environment variables with a customer managed AWS KMS key. By storing the sensitive configuration in encrypted environment variables and specifying the customer managed key, the values are protected, and enabling the encryption helpers ensures the variables are encrypted in transit during the API calls that set and retrieve them rather than being transmitted in plaintext. Granting the function's execution role permission to use the KMS key allows the function to decrypt the values at runtime, satisfying the encryption-in-transit requirement.

Option A is incorrect because a String type parameter in Parameter Store is not encrypted; you would need a SecureString, and a plain String parameter does not provide the KMS-based encryption described. Option B is incorrect because using a Lambda layer to retrieve secrets adds unnecessary architectural complexity and is not the targeted way to meet the stated requirement for encrypting configuration data with the created key. Option C is incorrect because storing each sensitive field as a separate encrypted S3 object adds significant retrieval overhead and complexity compared with using the native encrypted environment variable feature designed for exactly this purpose.

43.text:
Option C is correct because AWS SAM CLI is purpose-built to test serverless functions locally with minimal overhead. The sam local generate-event s3 put command produces a correctly structured sample S3 put-object event, and sam local invoke runs the function locally in a Docker container using that event payload. This lets the developer fully exercise the function against a realistic S3 event on their development machine before deploying to a production account, without provisioning any AWS resources.

Option A is incorrect because uploading to a real S3 bucket and expecting a local Lambda invocation does not work; an S3 event notification invokes a deployed function in AWS, not a function on the local machine. Option B is incorrect because aws lambda invoke calls a function already deployed in AWS rather than executing the code locally, so it does not satisfy local testing before publishing. Option D is incorrect because creating a test event in the AWS Management Console tests the function within the AWS environment, not on the local development machine, contradicting the local-testing requirement.

44.text:
Options B and D are correct because they describe the two halves of CloudFormation cross-stack references. The producing template (the network team's) must publish values it wants to share by declaring them in its Outputs section with an Export name; if the subnets are not exported, there is nothing for another stack to reference. The consuming template (the developer's) must then retrieve those exported values using the Fn::ImportValue intrinsic function. If the developer's template fails to use ImportValue, it cannot resolve the cross-stack subnet references, causing the launch to fail.

Option A is incorrect because the Ref intrinsic function references resources or parameters within the same template, not values exported from a different stack. Option C is incorrect because the Mappings section defines static key-value lookups within a template and is not used to reference subnets created by another stack. Option E is incorrect because exports for cross-stack references are declared in the Outputs section, not the Mappings section, so there is no concept of exporting subnets in Mappings.

45.text:
Option D is correct because a Lambda function can only reach an RDS DB instance in a private subnet if the function itself is connected to the VPC. Configuring the function with the VPC's private subnets attaches elastic network interfaces that give it private connectivity to resources in that VPC. You must also add security group rules that allow inbound traffic to the DB instance on its database port from the Lambda function's security group. The IAM role grants permissions, but network reachability requires this VPC and security group configuration.

Option A is incorrect because assigning a public IP and opening the security group to it exposes the database to the internet and contradicts the private-subnet design and security best practices. Option B is incorrect because AWS Direct Connect establishes a dedicated link between on-premises networks and AWS; it is irrelevant to connecting a Lambda function to a database within the same VPC. Option C is incorrect because CloudFront is a content delivery network for distributing web content, not a mechanism for connecting a Lambda function to a private database.

46.text:
Option B is correct because the requirement is to store the API key as part of the Lambda configuration while retaining full control over the encryption keys. Lambda environment variables are the configuration mechanism for the function, and Lambda encrypts them with KMS. By creating an AWS KMS customer managed key to encrypt the variable, the company has full control over the key, including its policy, rotation, and access, ensuring the encrypted API key is visible only to authorized entities that are permitted to use the key.

Option A is incorrect because using the default AWS-provided KMS key does not give the company full control over the key, which the requirement explicitly demands. Option C is incorrect because storing the API key in the code repository is insecure regardless of repository encryption and does not store the key as part of the Lambda configuration. Option D is incorrect because storing the key in a DynamoDB table encrypted with an AWS managed key neither places the key in the Lambda configuration nor provides the full key control required, since an AWS managed key is not customer-controlled.

47.text:
Option A is correct because the standard pattern for alerting on a term in CloudWatch Logs is to create a metric filter and alarm on it. You select the appropriate log group, define a metric filter with "ERROR" as the search term so that matching log events increment a custom CloudWatch metric, and then create a CloudWatch alarm that triggers when the metric value is 1 or higher. The alarm's action notifies the SNS topic, which delivers the email to the subscribed development team.

Option B is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs Insights is an interactive query tool for ad hoc analysis; you cannot create a standing metric and alarm directly from an Insights query for continuous notification. Option C is incorrect because there is no SNS subscription filter on a log group that emails based on a log pattern; SNS subscription filter policies apply to message attributes on SNS subscriptions, not to scanning log groups. Option D is incorrect because a CloudWatch alarm cannot be created directly with a log filter pattern and log group dimension; alarms act on metrics, so a metric filter must first convert the log pattern into a metric.

48.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to store secrets encrypted at rest with KMS and to rotate them automatically on a schedule. Storing the secrets in Secrets Manager, encrypting them with a customer master key, and enabling automatic rotation satisfies the monthly rotation and at-rest encryption requirements with built-in functionality rather than custom code. An EC2 user data script can then programmatically retrieve the secrets at instance startup and export them as environment variables, giving the application what it needs with the least development effort.

Option A is incorrect because storing secrets in a text file in S3 and attempting rotation via S3 Object Lambda is not a rotation mechanism and requires substantial custom logic, while plaintext-in-file handling weakens security. Option B is incorrect because Parameter Store standard parameters do not provide built-in scheduled secret rotation, so you must build and maintain a custom Lambda rotation function, which is more development effort than Secrets Manager's native rotation. Option C is incorrect because base64 encoding is encoding, not encryption, so it provides no real protection, and hand-written rotation scripts add fragile, undifferentiated effort.

49.text:
Option B is correct because a global secondary index (GSI) lets you query a DynamoDB table using an alternate partition key (and optional sort key) that differs from the base table's primary key. By creating a GSI with the slow-query attribute as the index partition key, the query becomes an efficient Query operation against the index rather than a full table Scan, and this performance scales as the table grows because DynamoDB indexes are partitioned and provisioned independently.

Option A is incorrect because increasing the Limit page size and retrying on throughput exceptions does not change the fact that querying a non-key attribute still requires scanning items; it only affects pagination, not query efficiency. Option C is incorrect because a parallel Scan reads the entire table across segments, which becomes slower and more expensive as data volume increases, the opposite of what is needed. Option D is incorrect because increasing read capacity units only provides more throughput for the existing access pattern; the underlying Scan on a non-key attribute remains inefficient and costly, so raising RCUs does not solve the latency problem and increases cost.

50.text:
Option C is correct because AWS SAM is implemented as a CloudFormation macro that requires a Transform section declaring AWS::Serverless-2016-10-09. This Transform tells CloudFormation to expand the shorthand SAM syntax (such as AWS::Serverless::Function and AWS::Serverless::Api) into full standard CloudFormation resources during deployment, satisfying both the SAM requirement and the requirement that all infrastructure deploy via CloudFormation templates.

Option A is incorrect because manually authoring AWS::Lambda::Function resources in the Resources section is plain CloudFormation, not AWS SAM, and forgoes the simplified SAM abstractions the team is required to use. Option B is incorrect because the Mappings section is for static key-value lookup tables and cannot contain resource definitions; SAM resource types are not valid there. Option D is incorrect because the Parameters section defines input values passed at stack creation and cannot host a SAM Globals section; Globals is a top-level SAM construct, and neither replaces the mandatory Transform declaration that enables SAM processing.

51.text:
Option A is correct because DefinitionSubstitutions on the AWS::StepFunctions::StateMachine resource lets you inject CloudFormation values, such as the API Gateway endpoint obtained through Ref or Fn::Sub, directly into placeholders within the Amazon States Language definition. This resolves the cross-resource reference at deployment time with no additional services, making it the most cost-effective solution because it incurs no extra charges.

Option B is incorrect because Step Functions state machines do not have environment variables like Lambda functions do; this concept does not exist for that resource type. Option C is incorrect because storing a non-secret API endpoint in AWS Secrets Manager adds monthly per-secret charges and API call costs, which is unnecessary overhead for a value that is not sensitive. Option D is incorrect because AWS AppConfig is designed for runtime feature flags and dynamic configuration deployment, introducing additional services, retrieval logic, and cost that are excessive for simply passing a static endpoint into a state machine at stack creation.

52.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Step Functions provides built-in error handling through the Retry field in the Amazon States Language definition. By adding a Retry block to the Task state that invokes the Lambda function and specifying the error type (such as States.Timeout or a Lambda timeout error) along with MaxAttempts, IntervalSeconds, and BackoffRate, the state machine automatically reattempts the failed invocation without any custom orchestration code.

Option B is incorrect because the Timeout field only defines how long a state may run before it is considered failed; it does not perform retries and has no concept of maximum retry attempts. Option C is incorrect because a Fail state explicitly terminates the execution as failed and cannot be configured with retry attempts; it is the opposite of what is required. Option D is incorrect because routing invocations through Amazon SNS to a subscribed Lambda function adds unnecessary architecture, and SNS retry behavior applies to delivery, not to re-executing a timed-out workflow step. The native Retry mechanism is the correct and simplest approach.

53.text:
Option D is correct because passing the log group name (or its ARN) as an environment variable on the AWS::Lambda::Function resource makes the value directly available to the function code at runtime through standard environment variable access. In the CloudFormation template you reference the log group resource with Ref or Fn::GetAtt and assign it to the Environment Variables map, so the application reads it without any extra lookups.

Option A is incorrect because the AWS::Include transform inserts reusable template snippets at deployment time and has nothing to do with delivering runtime values to a Lambda function. Option B is incorrect because user data is an EC2 instance bootstrap mechanism and is not applicable to Lambda functions. Option C is incorrect because the Mappings section provides static lookup values during template processing but does not expose data to the running Lambda function's execution environment; only environment variables (or a parameter store call) provide the value at runtime, making the environment variable approach the correct and simplest choice.

54.text:
Option A is correct because the ssm dynamic reference retrieves a plaintext value from AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store at stack create or update time. Using the syntax {{resolve:ssm:parameter-name:version}}, CloudFormation resolves the RDS hostname stored as a standard String parameter and injects it into the template, which is exactly the requirement for a plaintext parameter value.

Option B is incorrect because the Ref intrinsic function returns values of resources or parameters defined within the same template, not values stored externally in Parameter Store. Option C is incorrect because Fn::ImportValue retrieves outputs exported by other CloudFormation stacks, which is unrelated to Systems Manager parameters. Option D is incorrect because the ssm-secure dynamic reference is specifically for SecureString parameters that are KMS-encrypted; using it against a plaintext String parameter is inappropriate, and it is intended for sensitive data such as passwords rather than a non-sensitive hostname. The plain ssm reference correctly handles the plaintext parameter.

55.text:
Option A is correct because server-side encryption using an SQS managed key (SSE-SQS) encrypts the message bodies at rest within the queue with no additional configuration or key management. AWS handles the encryption keys transparently, so enabling SSE-SQS directly satisfies the requirement to encrypt all data at rest with the least operational effort.

Option B is incorrect because the aws:SecureTransport condition enforces encryption in transit (TLS) for requests to the queue; it does nothing to protect data at rest. Option C is incorrect because AWS Certificate Manager issues TLS certificates for securing connections and cannot be referenced when sending SQS messages, so it does not provide at-rest encryption. Option D is incorrect because message attributes are arbitrary metadata that SQS does not interpret; setting a name of ENCRYPT with a value of TRUE has no effect on how messages are stored and provides no actual encryption. Only enabling server-side encryption protects the message data while it resides in the queue.

56.text:
Option C is correct because defining an ECS task definition with the AWS Fargate launch type runs the container without provisioning or managing any EC2 infrastructure. Fargate is serverless, so AWS handles capacity, patching, and scaling, and the developer simply schedules the task (for example, hourly via an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule) to run the lightweight workload, giving the least infrastructure management overhead.

Option A is incorrect because adding a capacity provider tied to EC2 Auto Scaling still requires managing the underlying EC2 instances, including patching and right-sizing. Option B is incorrect because running the application on a dedicated EC2 instance means continuous instance management and paying for an instance that sits idle between hourly runs. Option D is incorrect because managed node groups are an Amazon EKS concept, not ECS, and even managed nodes still involve EC2 instance lifecycle management. Fargate eliminates all server management, making it the lowest-overhead option for this small, periodic task.

57.text:
Option D is correct because the aws ec2 run-instances command is the AWS CLI operation that launches one or more new EC2 instances from a specified AMI, instance type, and other configuration parameters. It maps to the RunInstances API action, which is the standard way to provision new instances programmatically.

Option A is incorrect because aws ec2 bundle-instance creates a bundle from an existing instance-store-backed Windows instance for AMI creation; it does not launch instances. Option B is incorrect because aws ec2 start-instances starts instances that already exist and are in a stopped state; it cannot create a brand-new instance. Option C is incorrect because aws ec2 confirm-product-instance only verifies whether a specified product code is associated with an existing instance, which is unrelated to launching one. Only run-instances actually creates and launches a new EC2 instance.

58.text:
Option D is correct because creating IAM resources with custom (explicit) names requires the developer to acknowledge CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM when deploying the stack. CloudFormation requires this specific capability whenever a template creates named IAM resources, and omitting it produces the InsufficientCapabilities error; specifying CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM resolves it.

Option A is incorrect because CAPABILITY_AUTO_EXPAND is required only when a template contains macros or nested transforms such as AWS SAM or AWS::Include, not for named IAM resources. Option B is incorrect because using an administrator role addresses permissions but does not satisfy the explicit capability acknowledgment that CloudFormation requires; the InsufficientCapabilities error would still occur regardless of the deploying principal's permissions. Option C is incorrect because CAPABILITY_IAM covers IAM resources only when they use auto-generated names; because these resources have custom names, the more specific CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM acknowledgment is mandatory.

59.text:
Option B is correct because the --disable-rollback option on the create-stack and update-stack commands tells CloudFormation not to roll back the stack when a resource fails to provision. With rollback disabled, the resources that were successfully created are preserved so the developer can inspect and troubleshoot them rather than having CloudFormation delete everything, exactly meeting the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because --enable-termination-protection only prevents the entire stack from being accidentally deleted; it does not control rollback behavior during a failed provisioning operation. Option C is incorrect because there is no built-in PreserveResources parameter in CloudFormation; passing an arbitrary parameter does nothing unless the template explicitly defines and acts on it. Option D is incorrect because tags are metadata key-value pairs attached to resources and have no effect on rollback or resource preservation during stack failures. Only disabling rollback keeps the already-provisioned resources intact when an error occurs.

60.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached is a multi-threaded, in-memory caching engine that scales out horizontally by adding nodes, making it well suited to offloading heavy read traffic from the RDS database. The technical lead specifically called for a multi-threaded and scalable caching solution, and Memcached's simple architecture provides this with the least complexity for caching frequently read query results.

Option B is incorrect because DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) only accelerates DynamoDB, not Amazon RDS, and migrating data to DynamoDB adds significant complexity. Option C is incorrect because an RDS Multi-AZ standby instance exists for high availability and failover and cannot serve read traffic; read replicas, not the standby, handle reads, so this does not meet the caching requirement. Option D is incorrect because while Redis is a capable cache, it is primarily single-threaded for command execution and is more feature-rich and complex than required; the requirement explicitly emphasizes a multi-threaded engine, which points to Memcached.

61.text:
Option A is correct because Lambda alias routing configuration supports weighted aliases, which let you point a single alias at two function versions and shift a fixed percentage of invocation traffic to the new version. This canary-style traffic shifting is configured directly on the alias and integrates with the company's existing in-house deployment process, satisfying the requirement to expose a new version to only a portion of production users.

Option B is incorrect because canary and linear deployment types are constructs of AWS CodeDeploy used to manage Lambda deployments, not a native Lambda configuration the company's in-house solution can simply set; the question asks for routing on the alias itself. Option C is incorrect because environment variables configure function behavior and cannot split or weight invocation traffic between versions. Option D is incorrect for the same reason as B: linear deployment is a CodeDeploy traffic-shifting strategy rather than the alias weighting mechanism described, and it shifts in increments rather than holding a fixed percentage.

62.text:
Option C is correct because before deploying with the AWS SAM CLI, the developer must package the application using sam package (or sam build followed by packaging). This step uploads local artifacts such as the Lambda deployment bundles to Amazon S3 and produces a packaged template with the artifact locations resolved, which is then used by sam deploy. Bundling via SAM package is the required prerequisite step.

Option A is incorrect because manually compressing code to a .zip file and uploading it to Lambda bypasses the SAM workflow and is not how SAM deployments operate. Option B is incorrect because tracing in AWS X-Ray is an observability feature for analyzing invocations after deployment, not a prerequisite for deploying. Option D is incorrect because eb create is an AWS Elastic Beanstalk CLI command for creating Beanstalk environments and is entirely unrelated to the AWS SAM deployment process for serverless Lambda applications.

63.text:
Option B is correct because in Amazon ECS, environment variables for a container are defined within the container definition that is part of the task definition. You specify an array of name-value pairs under the environment parameter, and ECS injects these variables into the container at startup, making them available to the application as it initializes on Fargate.

Option A is incorrect because the service definition controls how many tasks run and how they are scheduled, load balanced, and maintained; it does not define container-level environment variables. Option C is incorrect because entryPoint overrides the container's startup command (the executable to run), not its environment variables, so placing variables there would not set them in the environment. Option D is incorrect for both reasons combined: the service definition is the wrong location and entryPoint is the wrong parameter. Environment variables must be declared under the environment parameter in the task definition.

64.text:
Option B is correct because enabling a DynamoDB stream is only the first step; you must also create an event source mapping that connects the stream to the Lambda function. The event source mapping is the Lambda resource that polls the DynamoDB stream shards and invokes the function with batches of stream records, so without it the function will never be triggered by table updates even though the stream is active and permissions are correct.

Option A is incorrect because the StreamViewType only controls what data each record contains (keys only, new image, old image, or both) and does not affect whether the function is invoked. Option C is incorrect because DynamoDB streams cannot be mapped directly to an SNS topic, and adding SNS would not establish the polling integration Lambda needs. Option D is incorrect because the timeout setting governs how long an invocation may run, which is irrelevant when the function is not being invoked at all. Only configuring the event source mapping enables the trigger.

65.text:
Option C is correct because setting the event source mapping maximum concurrency lets you control how many concurrent Lambda invocations each SQS queue consumes. By assigning a maximum concurrency of 10 to the high priority queue and 90 to the low priority queue, the high priority queue is guaranteed up to 10 simultaneous invocations while the total stays within the 100-invocation limit, ensuring high priority work is processed without starving overall capacity.

Option A is incorrect because batch size controls how many messages are delivered per invocation, not how many concurrent invocations run, so it does not enforce the prioritization or concurrency cap described. Option B is incorrect because delivery delay only postpones when messages become visible and does not guarantee that the high priority queue is read first or limit concurrency. Option D is incorrect because the batch window controls how long Lambda waits to gather messages before invoking, again unrelated to concurrency limits. Only maximum concurrency settings allocate invocation capacity across the two queues as required.

66.text:
Option D is correct because the AWS CLI command syntax for get-item is valid, so the most likely cause of errors and no returned data is that the IAM user whose credentials are configured lacks an IAM policy granting read permission (such as dynamodb:GetItem) on demoman-table. Without that permission, DynamoDB returns an access-denied error and no items.

Option A is incorrect because put-item writes a new item rather than retrieving one; switching to it would not read data and is not what the developer intends. Option B is incorrect because AWS Support does not grant table access; access is controlled entirely through IAM policies that the account's own administrators manage. Option C is incorrect because DynamoDB is fully accessible through the AWS CLI, which itself calls the DynamoDB API, so claiming it must be called only via REST is false. The realistic explanation is missing IAM read permissions on the table.

67.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store provides hierarchical paths that let you organize variables such as the API URL and authentication information by environment, and the application retrieves them by path with minimal code changes using the SDK or built-in integrations. Storing the actual credentials in AWS Secrets Manager adds secure storage with automatic rotation for the most sensitive values, giving an appropriate separation between configuration and secrets across development, testing, and production.

Option B is incorrect because AWS KMS is a key management service for cryptographic keys and is not designed to store arbitrary configuration values like URLs and credentials. Option C is incorrect because embedding an encrypted file with the application requires redeploying the application to change any value and complicates rotation, which conflicts with making values available to future versions easily. Option D is incorrect because defining authentication information and URLs directly in the ECS task definition hardcodes them per deployment, requiring task definition revisions for every change and offering weaker secret handling than Parameter Store and Secrets Manager.

68.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to securely store credentials such as API keys, encrypts them with KMS, supports rotation, and is managed entirely through code via API and infrastructure-as-code. The application retrieves the API key at runtime with the AWS SDK and can cache it, so the integration does not affect application performance while keeping the secret out of source code, meeting the security requirement most strongly.

Option B is incorrect because hardcoding credentials in a code variable and committing them to a Git repository exposes the secret to anyone with repository access and is a serious security anti-pattern. Option C is incorrect because storing credentials as an S3 object provides no native rotation or fine-grained secret features and is not the dedicated secrets store, making it less secure and harder to manage as code. Option D is incorrect because DynamoDB is a general-purpose database lacking built-in encryption-by-default secret handling and rotation for credentials, so it is a weaker choice than the managed Secrets Manager service.

69.text:
Option D is correct because defining a single CloudFormation parameter for the instance type and restricting its acceptable input with the AllowedValues property enforces selection only from the approved list. When the stack is launched, CloudFormation presents and validates the choice against AllowedValues, rejecting any unapproved instance type, which cleanly governs the input across all accounts using one template.

Option A is incorrect because creating a separate template per instance type causes massive duplication and maintenance burden and does not constrain a single deployment to approved values. Option B is incorrect because creating a resource for each instance type would attempt to launch every type at once rather than letting the user pick one approved value. Option C is incorrect because creating a distinct parameter for each instance type is unnecessary and confusing; the user would still need a way to choose among them. A single parameter with AllowedValues is the standard, concise way to limit input to an approved set.

70.text:
Option D is correct because attaching an IAM policy to the Amazon Cognito identity pool's authenticated role and using policy variables such as ${cognito-identity.amazonaws.com:sub} to scope S3 prefixes restricts each user to their own folder. This enforces access control at the IAM level, so users obtain temporary credentials that can only read and write objects under their unique prefix, providing the highest level of security and working well for files up to 300 MB through direct S3 access.

Option A is incorrect because S3 Event Notifications fire after objects change and are used to trigger downstream processing, not to authorize or validate uploads and downloads. Option B is incorrect because filtering a file list in the UI by comparing user IDs is only a client-side display filter and does not actually prevent a user from accessing another user's objects. Option C is incorrect because routing all uploads and downloads through API Gateway and Lambda adds latency, cost, and payload limits, and relies on custom validation code that is more error-prone than enforcing isolation directly with IAM and Cognito.

71.text:
Option C is correct because when you register a Lambda function as a target for an Application Load Balancer, the ALB must have permission to invoke the function. Elastic Load Balancing needs a resource-based permission (an AWS Lambda Add Permission entry granting the elasticloadbalancing principal lambda:InvokeFunction); the console adds this automatically, but a CLI registration does not, so without it the ALB cannot invoke the function and requests are not processed.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda functions are fully supported as ALB targets within a dedicated target group. Option B is incorrect because registration is supported through the CLI, SDK, and CloudFormation, not only the Management Console; the console simply also adds the invoke permission for you. Option D is incorrect because cross-zone load balancing affects how traffic is distributed across Availability Zones and has nothing to do with whether the ALB is permitted to invoke a Lambda target. The missing invoke permission is the cause.

72.text:
Option A is correct because the AWS::Region pseudo parameter is automatically populated by CloudFormation with the Region in which the stack is being created. Referencing it requires no input, no extra services, and no string parsing, so it is the most operationally efficient way for the template to self-populate the deployment Region.

Option B is incorrect because requiring the Region as a CloudFormation parameter forces manual input on every deployment, which is error-prone and not self-populating. Option C is incorrect because extracting the Region from the AWS::StackId ARN with Fn::Split is an unnecessarily convoluted workaround when a dedicated pseudo parameter already provides the value directly. Option D is incorrect because dynamically importing the Region from Systems Manager Parameter Store introduces an external dependency and added complexity for information CloudFormation already exposes natively. The AWS::Region pseudo parameter is the simplest and most efficient choice.

73.text:
Option C is correct because VPC Flow Logs capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in a VPC, including traffic at the subnet level. By enabling flow logs for subnet B (or the relevant network interface), the developer can see ACCEPT or REJECT records that confirm whether on-premises packets are actually reaching subnet B and whether a security group or network ACL is dropping them. This directly answers the question of whether traffic arrives at the subnet.
