Option A is incorrect because VPN logs (such as Site-to-Site VPN tunnel logs) report on tunnel state and negotiation, not on whether traffic reached a specific subnet inside the VPC. Option B is incorrect because BGP relates to dynamic route advertisement over the VPN; BGP logs would show route exchange, not packet-level delivery to subnet B. Option D is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail records management API calls (control plane activity) against AWS services, not the data-plane network traffic flowing between on-premises and EC2 instances.

74.text:
Option A is correct because an EC2 instance assumes permissions through an IAM role attached via an instance profile. To allow the application to write objects to S3, the developer must modify the IAM policy attached to that instance profile role to include the necessary s3:PutObject permission on the target bucket. The SDK on the instance automatically retrieves temporary credentials from the instance metadata service based on this role's permissions.

Option B is incorrect because a session policy is an inline policy passed programmatically during an AssumeRole call to further restrict the session; it is not how an EC2 instance profile grants standing permissions, and it can only narrow, not expand, permissions. Option C is incorrect because a KMS key policy governs access to encryption keys, not the ability to write S3 objects; it would only matter if the bucket required a specific customer managed key, which is not the requirement here. Option D is incorrect because a VPC endpoint policy controls which actions are permitted through a Gateway or Interface endpoint, but it does not grant the instance the underlying IAM permission needed to call PutObject.

75.text:
Option B is correct because the secure pattern for encrypting data written to Lambda /tmp storage is envelope encryption using AWS KMS. The developer grants the function's execution role permission to call KMS, then uses GenerateDataKey to obtain a plaintext data key (and its encrypted copy) and encrypts the temporary data with that key before writing it to /tmp. This keeps the root key in KMS, leverages a managed and auditable key, and meets strict healthcare security requirements without exposing the master key material.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda does not use attachable Amazon EBS volumes; there is no EBS encryption setting in Lambda configuration, so this option describes a service capability that does not exist for /tmp. Option C is incorrect because generating a key with OpenSSL on startup provides no centralized key management, rotation, or auditing, and storing or deriving keys locally is far less secure than KMS. Option D is incorrect because routing every encryption request to an on-premises HSM adds latency, network dependency, and operational complexity that conflict with a serverless, highly available design when AWS KMS or CloudHSM in-region is available.

76.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon ElastiCache for Redis supports sorted sets and other rich data structures that let the application sort or rank cached datasets natively, which is the specific requirement. Redis also supports encryption in transit (TLS) and encryption at rest, satisfying the requirement that PHI be encrypted at all times while delivering high-performance caching of frequently accessed data.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached is a simple key-value store that does not provide sorting or ranking data structures, and historically it lacks the same encryption-at-rest and in-transit capabilities, so it cannot meet the sort/rank or encryption needs. Option C is incorrect because an RDS read replica scales read traffic but is not an in-memory cache and provides no native ranking of cached datasets; it also does not reduce latency the way an in-memory store does. Option D is incorrect because moving to DynamoDB with DAX would require redesigning the data store away from the existing encrypted RDS for MySQL database and still does not natively provide the sort/rank behavior over cached data that Redis sorted sets offer.

77.text:
Option B is correct because AWS CloudFormation StackSets are purpose-built to deploy the same stack across multiple Regions (and accounts) from a single template and operation. Using the create-stack-set command, the developer defines the load test resources once and provisions them consistently in every desired Region with no additional application code, which is exactly what geographic load testing of the API requires.

Option A is incorrect because writing and deploying a Lambda function per Region to invoke CloudFormation introduces custom code and orchestration overhead, contradicting the no-additional-code requirement. Option C is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager documents are designed for operational automation and instance management, not for declaratively provisioning a full set of stack resources across Regions, and would require additional scripting. Option D is incorrect because the CLI deploy command operates on a single stack in a single Region per invocation, so the developer would have to run it repeatedly and manage each Region individually rather than using the native multi-Region StackSets capability.

78.text:
Option D is correct because Amazon EventBridge can match CloudTrail management events in near real time. The developer creates a rule with an event pattern that filters CreateRole events whose request did not originate from CloudFormation, and sets the SNS topic as the rule's target. EventBridge invokes the target immediately when a matching event occurs, so the security team is notified right away with no custom compute to manage.

Options A, B, and C are all incorrect primarily because they rely on a polling schedule (every 15 minutes), which violates the requirement to notify the security team immediately and instead introduces up to a 15-minute delay. Beyond the timing problem, Option A adds an unnecessary Lambda function, Option B adds a Fargate task and ECS infrastructure, and Option C requires a continuously running EC2 instance with a cron job; all of these add significant operational overhead and cost compared to the fully managed, event-driven EventBridge-to-SNS pattern.

79.text:
Option A is correct because a single BatchGetItem operation can retrieve items from multiple tables in one API call, returning the requested songs and artists together. The developer supplies the songName/artistName composite keys for the songs table and the artistName keys for the artists table within the same request. This minimizes network round trips and delivers optimal performance because all the specific items are fetched by their primary keys in one operation.

Option B is incorrect because creating an LSI and issuing a separate query per artistName generates many calls and added complexity, increasing network traffic rather than reducing it. Option C is incorrect because performing two separate BatchGetItem calls (one per table) doubles the number of API round trips when a single multi-table BatchGetItem would suffice. Option D is incorrect because Scan reads the entire table and then filters client-side, which is the most expensive and slowest pattern; it consumes far more read capacity and network bandwidth than directly retrieving known items by key.

80.text:
Option B is correct because AMI IDs are Region-specific, so an AMI created in us-east-1 does not exist in us-west-1, which is why the stack creation fails. The lowest-overhead fix is to copy the custom AMI from us-east-1 to us-west-1 using the built-in CopyImage capability, then update the us-west-1 CloudFormation template to reference the new AMI ID and relaunch the stack. This reuses the existing image without rebuilding it.

Option A is incorrect because switching both Regions to a generic AWS-provided AMI would discard the company's custom application image and require reconfiguring the application, which is a major change rather than a fix. Option C is incorrect because rebuilding the custom AMI from scratch in us-west-1 and authoring a new template is significantly more effort than copying the existing AMI. Option D is incorrect because deploying manually outside CloudFormation abandons the company's infrastructure-as-code approach, increases operational overhead, and creates configuration drift between Regions.

81.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon Macie is the AWS service that uses machine learning and pattern matching to discover sensitive data in Amazon S3, including financial data such as credit card numbers. The developer runs a Macie job against the affected S3 buckets and filters on the SensitiveData:S3Object/Financial finding type, which specifically surfaces objects containing financial information like credit card details, allowing all potential exposures to be identified.

Options A and D are incorrect because Amazon Athena is an interactive query service for analyzing data with SQL; it does not natively classify or detect sensitive data and does not produce SensitiveData finding types. Option C is incorrect because, although it correctly uses Macie, the SensitiveData:S3Object/Personal finding type targets personally identifiable information rather than financial data; credit card information is classified under the Financial finding type, so filtering on Personal would miss the relevant exposures.

82.text:
Option B is correct because the too many connections errors occur even though CPU and memory are healthy, indicating the database has exhausted its connection limit due to Lambda concurrency opening many short-lived connections. Amazon RDS Proxy solves this by pooling and reusing database connections across concurrent Lambda invocations, dramatically reducing the number of open connections to the database. Initializing the connection outside the handler also lets the function reuse a connection across warm invocations.

Option A is incorrect because raising max_user_connections and restarting the cluster causes downtime and only postpones the limit; it does not address the underlying connection storm from scaling Lambda. Options C and D are incorrect because introducing SQS FIFO queues changes the architecture to asynchronous processing, which breaks the requirement to return order confirmation immediately. Throttling Lambda concurrency to match available connections (as in C and D) would also serialize and delay order processing and add operational complexity, whereas RDS Proxy preserves real-time responses while safely managing connections.

83.text:
Option A is correct because BatchGetItem retrieves multiple specific items, identified by their primary keys, from one or more DynamoDB tables in a single API call. Because it reads only the exact items requested by key, it consumes the least read capacity and has the minimum impact on the table compared with operations that read more data than needed.

Option B is incorrect because GetItem retrieves only a single item per call, so fetching multiple items would require multiple API calls rather than one. Option C is incorrect because Scan reads every item in the table and then applies any filtering afterward, consuming the most read capacity and placing the heaviest load on the database. Option D is incorrect because Query retrieves items sharing a partition key and is not designed to fetch an arbitrary set of specific items across different keys in one request; it also reads more than just the targeted items, making BatchGetItem the more efficient and lower-impact choice.

84.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon ElastiCache provides a fast, in-memory, distributed data store that is purpose-built for externalizing session state away from individual application servers. This makes the application fault tolerant and natively highly scalable: because session data lives in a shared, replicated cache, any application instance can serve any user, and the failure of an instance does not interrupt the user experience.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network for caching and distributing content at edge locations, not a session state store. Option C is incorrect because, while Amazon S3 is durable, it is object storage with higher latency than an in-memory store and is not the recommended pattern for high-throughput session reads and writes. Option D is incorrect because enabling session stickiness on the load balancer ties a user to a single instance; if that instance fails, the user's session is lost, which directly violates the requirement that service interruptions not affect the user experience.

85.text:
Option A is correct because the most secure approach grants the CloudFormation service role only the specific S3 permissions it needs (ListBucket and GetObject) and adds a bucket policy that explicitly lists the trusted account numbers as the AWS principal. This scopes access to known accounts following least privilege, ensuring only the intended cross-account CloudFormation deployments can read the Lambda code.

Option B is incorrect because setting the bucket policy principal to "*" grants access to everyone, which is insecure and violates least privilege even if the role itself is limited. Options C and D are incorrect because they reference a service-based (service-linked) link to grant the Lambda function S3 permissions; service-linked roles are predefined by a service and are not used this way, and it is CloudFormation, not the Lambda function, that needs to retrieve the code to deploy it. Option D additionally uses a resource of "*", which is overly permissive, while Option C misapplies the mechanism entirely.

86.text:
Options A and C are correct because AWS CodePipeline supports change-driven source stages from both Amazon S3 and AWS CodeCommit. With S3 as the source, the pipeline can be triggered automatically when a new object version is uploaded, and with CodeCommit, the pipeline starts immediately when a change is committed (via EventBridge events). Both approaches build and deploy the application as soon as the source code changes, meeting the requirement for immediate, automatic deployment.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon EBS is block storage attached to an EC2 instance and is not a supported CodePipeline source; CodePipeline cannot detect file-level changes on an EBS volume. Option D is incorrect because polling every 15 minutes is time-based, not change-based, so it does not trigger immediately when the source changes. Option E is incorrect because EC2 ephemeral instance storage is not a CodePipeline source provider, and requiring an instance to start the pipeline adds unnecessary, unsupported custom infrastructure.

87.text:
Option C is correct because an S3 Lifecycle rule lets the developer configure an expiration action that automatically deletes objects a specified number of days after creation. Setting expiration to 90 days removes obsolete PDFs without any custom code, scheduling, or compute, which satisfies the requirement with the least development effort. The lifecycle engine is fully managed by Amazon S3.

Option A is incorrect because modifying application code to scan and delete objects daily requires writing, testing, and maintaining custom logic and a scheduling mechanism, which is far more effort than a built-in lifecycle rule. Option B is incorrect because creating a Lambda function plus a daily trigger still means authoring and operating custom code, adding unnecessary overhead. Option D is incorrect because repartitioning objects under a date-based key prefix and building a Lambda function to delete by prefix is the most complex option, involving both data reorganization and custom code, none of which is needed when native lifecycle expiration handles the task.

88.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon EFS is a fully managed, shared file system that can be mounted concurrently by multiple Lambda functions, other AWS services, and on-premises resources (over Direct Connect or VPN). Mounting EFS in the VPC-enabled Lambda function lets it store the result files and the shared log file in a common mount point and append new log entries directly to the existing file, satisfying both the file-sharing and append-in-place requirements.

Option B is incorrect because EBS volumes cannot be attached to Lambda functions, and even EBS Multi-Attach is limited to specific EC2 instance types in one Availability Zone, so it cannot provide shared access for Lambda or on-premises resources. Options C and D are incorrect because the Lambda /tmp directory and the /opt directory are local and ephemeral to a single function execution environment; they are not shared across functions, services, or on-premises systems, and they cannot serve as a durable, concurrently appended shared log file.

89.text:
Option B is correct because the NoSuchBucket error in a new account indicates that the AWS CDK has not yet provisioned its bootstrap resources there. Running cdk bootstrap creates the required staging resources, including the Amazon S3 bucket used to upload assets such as Lambda function code, in the target account and Region. Since assets must be staged before deployment, bootstrapping the beta account resolves the missing-bucket error so the stack can deploy.

Option A is incorrect because cdk synth only produces the CloudFormation template from the CDK app locally and does not create the asset bucket in the target account. Option C is incorrect because cdk init scaffolds a brand-new CDK project on the local machine and has nothing to do with preparing an account for deployment. Option D is incorrect because cdk destroy tears down a previously deployed stack; it would not create the bootstrap bucket and is irrelevant to a first-time deployment failure.

90.text:
Option B is correct because the bottleneck is repeated work: the overloaded on-premises database is queried and the same English newsletters are re-translated on every request. Caching the already-translated newsletters in the Lambda execution environment's /tmp directory lets warm invocations reuse the stored results instead of re-querying the database and re-calling Amazon Translate, which directly improves the function's response time without changing the database.

Option A is incorrect because switching to asynchronous invocation changes the invocation model and would not return the translated newsletter back to the browser request, nor does it reduce the database or translation load. Option C is incorrect because the Amazon Translate TranslateText API does not offer a built-in caching feature that the developer can simply enable. Option D is incorrect because parallel processing within the function does not reduce the number of expensive database queries or translation calls; the underlying repeated, redundant work remains the cause of the slow responses.

91.text:
Option B is correct because the AWS Encryption SDK uses envelope encryption: it requests a data key from the configured keyring or master key provider, encrypts the data with the plaintext data key, and then stores the encrypted (wrapped) data key alongside the ciphertext in the SDK's formatted message. Because the encrypted data key travels with the ciphertext, the developer does not have to track data keys separately; decryption simply uses the wrapping key to unwrap the stored data key.

Option A is incorrect because manually tracking data keys per object is exactly the burden the Encryption SDK eliminates by embedding the encrypted key in the message. Option C is incorrect because the SDK does not automatically store data keys in Amazon S3; the encrypted key is part of the returned ciphertext, not an external object. Option D is incorrect because EC2 user data is metadata for instance bootstrapping and is unrelated to how the Encryption SDK manages data keys; storing key material there would also be insecure.

92.text:
Options B and E are correct because the lowest-overhead path to run a containerized proof-of-concept is to package the application as a container image with the Docker CLI and push it to Amazon ECR, then run it on Amazon ECS with the AWS Fargate launch type. ECR provides a managed image registry, and ECS on Fargate runs containers serverlessly without the developer provisioning, patching, or scaling any EC2 instances, minimizing operational effort.

Option A is incorrect because packaging into a .zip file and uploading to S3 is a non-container deployment artifact (typical of Lambda or Elastic Beanstalk), which does not fit a container-based model. Option C is incorrect because deploying to EC2 with CodeDeploy requires managing instances and is not a container workflow. Option D is incorrect because, while EKS on Fargate also runs containers, operating a Kubernetes control plane and its constructs adds substantially more complexity than ECS on Fargate for a simple proof-of-concept, so it does not meet the least-operational-effort requirement.

93.text:
Option B is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built to store database credentials encrypted at rest with AWS KMS and to rotate them automatically, including native, managed rotation for Amazon RDS that updates both the secret and the database password. The EC2 instances retrieve credentials from Secrets Manager at runtime, so nothing is hardcoded in the application, meeting the security and automatic-rotation requirements.

Option A is incorrect because, while IAM database authentication avoids storing passwords, the question explicitly asks to store and access credentials with rotation, and SQL Server is not supported by RDS IAM database authentication, so it does not satisfy the stated requirement. Option C is incorrect because storing credentials in an S3 object and downloading them at launch provides no built-in automatic credential rotation and adds custom logic that is less secure. Option D is incorrect because building a DynamoDB table plus a custom Lambda and CloudWatch Events rotation mechanism reinvents functionality that Secrets Manager already provides securely and with less operational risk.

94.text:
Option B is correct because the symptom that the developer's personal AWS CLI credentials work on the same instance while the application fails proves the instance role and metadata service are functioning. The most likely cause is that the application still has the old IAM user's access keys present in environment variables; the AWS SDK's credential provider chain checks environment variables before the instance profile, so it keeps using the now-deleted user's disabled keys and receives AccessDenied.

Option A is incorrect because IAM policy propagation delays are brief and would not persist as a consistent AccessDenied after restart, and the personal account working rules this out. Option C is incorrect because the AWS SDK for Python (boto) fully supports credentials obtained from an EC2 instance role through the metadata service. Option D is incorrect because if the metadata endpoint at 169.254.169.254 were unreachable, the SDK could not retrieve role credentials at all, but more importantly security groups control inbound/outbound network traffic and do not block access to the link-local instance metadata service.

95.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon EFS is a shared, POSIX-compliant file system that multiple Lambda functions can mount concurrently through mount targets in the central VPC. Because the separate teams can independently write and update the libraries and reference data on the same file system, and the Lambda functions mount it (with the execution roles granted EFS access), this provides shared, continuously updatable access to both the data science libraries and reference data.

Option A is incorrect because EBS volumes cannot be attached to Lambda functions, and EBS Multi-Attach is restricted to specific EC2 instances in a single Availability Zone, so it cannot serve Lambda. Option B is incorrect because compressing files into the ephemeral /tmp folder makes the data local to each invocation and static at deployment time, preventing the teams from updating data independently. Option D is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Windows File Server uses SMB and targets Windows workloads; it is not the native, simply mountable shared file system for Lambda the way EFS is, making it a poorer fit for this Linux-based serverless scenario.

96.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is the most secure managed solution for database credentials: it encrypts secrets at rest with AWS KMS and supports automatic, scheduled rotation. By creating a rotation Lambda from the provided SecretsManagerRotationTemplate, the developer enables rotation for RDS, Amazon DocumentDB, and Aurora credentials, satisfying the policy that credentials be encrypted at rest and rotated at regular intervals across all these database services.

Option A is incorrect because IAM database authentication uses short-lived tokens rather than storing and rotating database credentials, and it is not uniformly supported across all the listed engines, so it does not meet the requirement to securely store and rotate credentials. Option B is incorrect because Systems Manager Parameter Store SecureString parameters are encrypted but do not provide built-in automatic credential rotation; rotation would require custom tooling. Option C is incorrect because storing credentials as an encrypted S3 object provides no native credential rotation, and S3 server-side encryption rotates the encryption key, not the database credentials themselves, leaving the actual secret unrotated.

97.text:
Option A is correct because a Lambda layer is purpose-built for packaging libraries, dependencies, and other shared content separately from the function's deployment package. By placing the 100 MB external library in a layer, the developer keeps the function's own package small, makes the library available in the execution environment under /opt, and can reuse the layer across multiple functions. This is a fully managed capability that requires no additional infrastructure, so it meets the requirement with the least operational overhead.

Option B is incorrect because Lambda cannot natively mount an S3 bucket as a file system; achieving this would require custom tooling and adds operational complexity. Option C is incorrect because /tmp is ephemeral scratch space initialized empty for each new execution environment, so a library cannot be reliably loaded there at deployment time, and using it still does not reduce the package size. Option D is incorrect because while EFS can be mounted to Lambda, it requires VPC configuration, mount targets, and access points, introducing significantly more setup and ongoing management than a simple layer.

98.text:
Option B is correct because AWS IAM Identity Center permission sets provide centralized, federated access management across multiple AWS accounts. The team can define a permission set once and assign it to developers, who then obtain temporary credentials to reach CodeCommit repositories in any of the accounts. This scales cleanly to a distributed, growing team and avoids the burden of managing individual long-lived credentials, making it the most operationally efficient approach.

Option A is incorrect because configuring IAM roles for each developer and granting access individually does not scale; it creates substantial administrative overhead as the team grows across accounts. Option C is incorrect because sharing AWS access keys is a serious security anti-pattern, providing no individual accountability and exposing long-lived credentials. Option D is incorrect because relying on public SSH keys for authentication still requires per-user, per-account key management and does not provide the centralized, federated access that Identity Center delivers.

99.text:
Option A is correct because AWS Secrets Manager has a built-in multi-Region replication feature. The developer enables replication on each secret, adds us-east-1 as a replica Region, and specifies a KMS key in the destination Region to encrypt the replicated secret. Secrets Manager then keeps the read-only replica synchronized with the primary secret automatically, including value and rotation updates, which precisely satisfies the requirement to replicate the us-west-1 secrets to us-east-1.

Option B is incorrect because replication is configured on the source secret, not by creating a new secret in the destination and pointing it back, and the replica must be encrypted with a key in the destination Region, not us-west-1. Option C is incorrect because Secrets Manager replication is continuous and automatic, not a rule that runs only during rotation. Option D is incorrect because Secrets Manager does not offer an S3-based lifecycle replication mechanism, and exporting secrets to S3 buckets is an insecure, unnecessary workaround.

100.text:
Option C is correct because Amazon DynamoDB provides a durable, highly available, and scalable external store for session state. By keeping session data in DynamoDB rather than on the EC2 instance, the application becomes stateless, so if any instance fails, no sessions are lost and another instance can immediately serve the request using the centrally stored session data. This is the standard pattern for fault-tolerant session handling.

Option A is incorrect because sticky sessions bind a user to a specific instance; if that instance fails, the session data stored locally on it is lost. Option B is incorrect because Amazon SQS is a message queue for decoupling components, not a session store, and is not designed for keyed session retrieval. Option D is incorrect because connection draining (deregistration delay) only allows in-flight requests to complete gracefully during deregistration; it does not persist or recover session data when an instance fails.

101.text:
Option D is correct because creating a global secondary index (GSI) with OrderSource as the partition key lets the application run an efficient Query operation that targets only the items where OrderSource equals MobileApp. A Query against a partition key reads just the matching items rather than scanning the entire table, which is essential and far more efficient given the table holds more than 100,000 records. A GSI also supports a partition key different from the base table's key, which a query requires.

Option A is incorrect because a Scan reads every item in the table and then filters, consuming large amounts of read capacity and scaling poorly as the table grows. Option B is incorrect because a local secondary index must share the base table's partition key (OrderID) and only adds an alternate sort key, so it cannot use OrderSource as its partition key. Option C is incorrect because a query keys primarily on the partition key; defining OrderSource only as the GSI sort key without an appropriate partition key does not enable querying all MobileApp records efficiently.

102.text:
Options B and E are correct because together they ensure end-to-end encryption in transit. Setting the Viewer Protocol Policy to Redirect HTTP to HTTPS forces clients to connect to CloudFront over TLS, encrypting traffic between viewers and the distribution. Setting the Origin Protocol Policy to Match Viewer makes CloudFront connect to the ALB origin using the same protocol the viewer used; combined with viewer redirection to HTTPS, this guarantees CloudFront-to-ALB traffic is also encrypted, protecting customer data outside the VPC on both legs of the connection.

Option A is incorrect because signed URLs restrict access to content for authorization purposes; they do not provide encryption in transit. Option C is incorrect because field-level encryption protects specific sensitive data fields at the application layer rather than securing the overall transport channel, and it is not the requested transit encryption configuration. Option D is incorrect because automatic object compression reduces payload size to improve performance and has no bearing on encrypting traffic.

103.text:
Option B is correct because an EC2 instance retrieves temporary credentials for its attached IAM role from the Instance Metadata Service, and those credentials are automatically refreshed. When you add a permission to the role's policy, the change propagates and takes effect for the running application without any restart or relaunch, providing the least possible disruption to the application.

Option A is incorrect because terminating the instance and launching a new one is highly disruptive and entirely unnecessary, since role policy changes apply to running instances automatically. Option C is incorrect because hibernating and restarting the instance causes downtime and is not needed for the new permission to take effect. Option D is incorrect because S3 read access for the application is granted through the IAM role's identity-based policy, not by adding a permission to the bucket alone, and restarting the EC2 instance is unnecessary.

104.text:
Option B is correct because the AWS AppConfig Agent Lambda extension is the purpose-built, low-effort way to give Lambda functions access to dynamic configuration. The extension runs as a separate process within the execution environment, retrieves the configuration from AppConfig, caches it locally, and periodically polls for updates. The function code simply makes a local HTTP call to the extension's localhost endpoint to read the latest 6 KB JSON document, so configuration can be changed in AppConfig and picked up at runtime without redeploying the application.

Option A is incorrect because moving the document into a Lambda environment variable hardcodes the configuration, and changing it requires updating and redeploying the function, which violates the no-redeployment requirement. Option C is incorrect because AWS X-Ray is a distributed tracing service and has no role in retrieving AppConfig data; its SDK does not call AppConfig APIs. Option D is incorrect because bundling the configuration file into the deployment package makes it static, so any change would require repackaging and redeploying the function.

105.text:
Option A is correct because AWS CloudTrail records management events for API activity across the account, including the DeleteDBInstance call made against Amazon RDS. By retrieving the CloudTrail events for the mysql-db resource where the event name is DeleteDBInstance, the developer can inspect each event's userIdentity element to see exactly which IAM user or role performed the deletion. CloudTrail retains 90 days of event history in Event history, matching the timeframe in this scenario.

Option B is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs capture database engine and application logs, not the API call identity or the actor who deleted the instance. Option C is incorrect because AWS X-Ray provides distributed tracing for application requests and does not record who invoked AWS control-plane API actions. Option D is incorrect because AWS Systems Manager does not maintain a deletions inventory of RDS resources, so no such filterable deletion details exist to identify the responsible principal.

106.text:
Options A and C are correct because they combine to invoke the Lambda function on object upload. Amazon S3 publishes object-created events to Amazon EventBridge, so creating an EventBridge rule with a pattern matching the S3 Object Created event (filtering for the .csv suffix) detects the upload. Adding a trigger to the existing Lambda function with the trigger type set to EventBridge and selecting that rule wires the rule to invoke the function, so the function runs automatically whenever a matching .csv file is uploaded.

Option B is incorrect because a scheduled EventBridge rule fires on a time interval rather than in response to an actual upload, introducing latency and unnecessary scanning. Option D is incorrect because writing a separate function to scan the bucket for recently added objects is a polling workaround that adds complexity and is not event-driven. Option E is incorrect because S3 Lifecycle rules manage object transitions and expiration between storage classes; they cannot invoke a Lambda function on upload.

107.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Lambda allocates CPU power proportionally to the amount of memory configured for the function. Increasing the allocated memory therefore also increases vCPU and overall compute capacity, which directly speeds up a CPU-bound image processing task. This is the standard performance-tuning lever for Lambda and lets the function complete well within the desired time.

Option A is incorrect because Lambda is serverless and does not expose instance types or let you choose a compute-optimized instance with a specific vCPU count; you control performance through the memory setting. Option B is incorrect because simply switching to the latest Python runtime offers no guarantee of meaningful speedup for this compute-heavy workload and does not address the underlying lack of allocated compute. Option D is incorrect because reserved concurrency limits or guarantees how many concurrent instances can run; it controls scaling and throttling, not the execution speed of an individual invocation.

108.text:
Option A is correct because Username is unique per request (each user can submit only one request) and has high cardinality, so using it as the partition key spreads writes evenly across many partitions. This well-distributed key avoids hot partitions during the expected surge of submissions, allowing DynamoDB to scale throughput across partitions and handle the burst of traffic efficiently.

Option B is incorrect because the submission date concentrates many writes onto the same or few values during the launch window, creating a hot partition that throttles the surge. Option C is incorrect because validation status has only three possible values (UNVALIDATED, VALID, NOT VALID), giving extremely low cardinality and forcing most items into a tiny number of partitions. Option D is incorrect because the rating is a 1 to 5 scale with only five possible values, which similarly produces severe key collisions and poor distribution under heavy load.

109.text:
Option C is correct because deploying the SAM application to a separate, dedicated development AWS account fully isolates the developer's one-time test from the shared pre-production environment. Using the AWS SAM CLI to package and deploy into the new account creates an independent CloudFormation stack with its own Lambda function and SNS topics, so the changes can be validated without any impact on the pre-production application that other team members rely on for the release pipeline.

Option A is incorrect because deploying into the pre-production account, even with a debug parameter, modifies the very environment that other team members are actively using. Option B is incorrect because creating a change set against pre-production and then executing it in a different account is not how change sets work; a change set is bound to the stack and account where it was created. Option D is incorrect because updating the pre-production stack to add a stage still alters the in-use stack and risks disrupting the existing application.

110.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Certificate Manager is a regional service, and an ACM certificate can only be associated with an Application Load Balancer in the same Region as the certificate. Therefore the developer must create a separate certificate in ACM in each of the three Regions and associate the local certificate with that Region's ALB. This satisfies the requirement to deploy SSL certificates to every ALB across all three Regions.

Option A is incorrect because ACM certificates cannot be exported and imported into ALBs in other Regions; an ACM-issued certificate is bound to its Region. Option B is incorrect because ACM does not offer a global certificate type that can be deployed to ALBs in multiple Regions. Option D is incorrect because the us-east-1 requirement for a single global certificate applies to Amazon CloudFront, not to regional Application Load Balancers, so a us-east-1 certificate cannot be attached to ALBs in other Regions.

111.text:
Options B and C are correct because they implement the AWS best practice for granting an EC2-hosted application secure access to S3 without static credentials. Creating an IAM role with permissions to the S3 bucket, then placing that role in an instance profile and attaching the instance profile to the EC2 instance, allows the application to obtain automatically rotated temporary credentials from the instance metadata service. This avoids storing or distributing long-lived access keys, which is the most secure approach.

Option A is incorrect because creating an IAM user and adding it to a group still relies on long-lived user credentials that must be managed and are less secure than role-based temporary credentials. Option D is incorrect because IAM roles are not assigned to IAM groups; roles are attached to instances via instance profiles, so this step is technically invalid. Option E is incorrect because storing IAM user credentials in environment variables on the instance exposes long-lived secrets and is a credential-management anti-pattern.

112.text:
Option D is correct because AWS Secrets Manager is the purpose-built, fully managed service for securely storing database credentials and natively supports automatic, scheduled rotation. Migrating the Aurora credentials to Secrets Manager, encrypting them with an AWS KMS key, enabling built-in rotation, and using IAM roles to grant the ECS Fargate tasks permission to retrieve the secret eliminates the in-application credential management and meets the security and rotation requirements with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because RDS parameter groups configure database engine settings, not credential storage, and they do not provide secret rotation. Option B is incorrect because Systems Manager Parameter Store does not provide native automatic credential rotation for database secrets the way Secrets Manager does, so it cannot satisfy the rotation requirement without additional custom work. Option C is incorrect because storing credentials in ECS Fargate environment variables exposes them in the task definition and provides no built-in rotation, which is less secure and higher overhead than Secrets Manager.

113.text:
Option C is correct because specifying a local cache in AWS CodeBuild and adding the dependency cache folder path to the cache section of the buildspec file lets CodeBuild persist downloaded dependencies on the build host between builds. Reusing the cached dependencies means subsequent builds retrieve far fewer dependencies from CodeArtifact, directly reducing transfer time and improving build performance for these frequent, dependency-heavy builds.

Option A is incorrect because while an S3 cache also helps, the question's solution centers on caching the local dependency folder path; moreover, simply naming an S3 cache without caching the right dependency directory does not by itself reduce retrievals, and the correct configuration is to cache the dependency folder. Option B is incorrect because a cache is configured by specifying folder paths to cache, not by adding the CodeArtifact repository name to the buildspec. Option D is incorrect because buildspec files are not retrieved from CodeArtifact, and referencing a repository name there does not enable dependency caching.

114.text:
Option A is correct because adding a global secondary index with customer_type as the partition key and email_address as the sort key lets the developer run a Query that targets a specific customer_type and uses the begins_with key condition expression on the email_address sort key to return partial prefix matches. A GSI can be added to an existing table without recreating it, and querying on the partition key with a begins_with condition on the sort key is the efficient, supported pattern for this autocomplete-style search.

Option B is incorrect because making email_address the partition key requires an exact match on it, which prevents partial-match searches and does not let you query by customer_type. Option C is incorrect because a local secondary index must use the base table's partition key (email_address), so it cannot use customer_type as its partition key, and LSIs can only be created at table creation time. Option D is incorrect because keying on job_title does not let the application search by customer_type as required.

115.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Secrets Manager natively supports automatic, scheduled rotation of database credentials, and a daily rotation schedule easily satisfies the requirement to rotate at least weekly. Storing the database user name and password in a Secrets Manager secret with rotation enabled lets the application retrieve current credentials at runtime while Secrets Manager handles rotating them and updating the database, providing a secure, managed solution.

Option A is incorrect because rotating the KMS key that encrypts a Systems Manager Parameter Store secure string does not rotate the database credentials themselves; the user name and password remain unchanged. Option B is incorrect because RDS for Microsoft SQL Server does not support IAM database authentication (that feature is for MySQL and PostgreSQL engines), so this approach is not viable. Option D is incorrect because creating the user via EC2 user data and passing the credentials in environment variables provides no rotation and exposes the static credentials, failing the security requirement.

116.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon CloudFront sits in front of the API and can cache responses at edge locations, reducing repeated load on API Gateway and Lambda for the same frequently repeated query. By configuring the CloudFront cache and having the application return cacheable content keyed on the relevant request attributes, identical requests are served from the edge cache, optimizing API resources for the high volume of duplicate queries.

Option B is incorrect because the API Gateway cache only caches responses for GET method requests; you cannot enable caching for the POST method in an API Gateway stage. Option C is incorrect because the Lambda /tmp directory is ephemeral and local to a single execution environment, so it cannot serve as a reliable shared cache across concurrent invocations or repeated requests. Option D is incorrect because Systems Manager Parameter Store is intended for configuration and secrets, not for caching API responses, and adds latency and operational complexity while not being a caching service.

117.text:
Option B is correct because the most effective pattern is to store each employee's structured contact details in Amazon DynamoDB for fast, indexed search and retrieval, while storing the large high-resolution photos as objects in Amazon S3 and saving only the S3 object keys (pointers) in the DynamoDB items. S3 is designed for durable, cost-effective storage of large binary objects, and both DynamoDB and S3 are accessible through AWS APIs, enabling efficient lookup of details and retrieval of the associated photos.

Option A is incorrect because Base64-encoding high-resolution photos and storing them in DynamoDB would frequently exceed the 400 KB item size limit and is expensive and inefficient for large binary data. Option C is incorrect because Amazon Cognito user pools provide authentication and user identity management, not a general-purpose store for contact details and photos. Option D is incorrect because pairing RDS with EFS for photo storage adds relational database and file system management overhead and is a less natural, more operationally heavy fit than the DynamoDB plus S3 pattern.

118.text:
Option C is correct because AWS CloudFormation is the infrastructure as code service designed to define resources in a reusable template (JSON or YAML) that can be deployed repeatedly to create identical environments. Using CloudFormation helper scripts such as cfn-init and cfn-hup, the template can install the necessary software and start the application on the EC2 instances, and CloudFormation supports updates and rollback to previous configurations, satisfying all the stated requirements.

Option A is incorrect because AWS Amplify is geared toward building and deploying web and mobile front-end and serverless backend applications, not provisioning and configuring EC2 instance infrastructure as repeatable templates. Option B is incorrect because AWS CodeBuild is a build service for compiling and testing code; the AppSpec file belongs to CodeDeploy, and CodeBuild does not create identical infrastructure environments or provide template-based rollback. Option D is incorrect because AWS AppSync is a managed GraphQL API service and does not deploy or version application infrastructure on EC2 instances.

119.text:
Option A is correct because AWS CodeBuild has native test reporting: by defining a reports section in the buildspec file that points to the test result files and their format, CodeBuild ingests the results and produces a test report you can view in the CodeBuild console. Adding this CodeBuild project to the pipeline means each build runs the tests and generates the test report, directly meeting the requirement.

Option B is incorrect because AWS CodeDeploy and its AppSpec file manage application deployment lifecycle hooks, not test report generation during builds. Option C is incorrect because running builds on an EC2 instance with the SSM Agent installed provides instance management capabilities but does not natively produce CodeBuild-style test reports within the pipeline. Option D is incorrect because AWS CodeArtifact is an artifact and package repository service; it does not offer a test report template or generate test reports.

120.text:
Option D is correct because enabling Amazon CloudWatch Logs for the API Gateway stages and logging requests in a structured JSON format, combined with the Lambda function logs that already flow to CloudWatch, centralizes the application's logs in CloudWatch Logs. CloudWatch Logs Insights then lets the developer run interactive queries across all these log groups to search for and pinpoint the errors, providing a fully managed search and analysis capability with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because API Gateway and Athena are not the right combination here; manually publishing logs via PutMetricData and querying through Athena adds unnecessary setup and operational burden. Option B is incorrect because CloudTrail records API management events, not application error logs, and routing them to S3 for Athena queries is more complex and does not capture the application-level errors. Option C is incorrect because building a Kinesis Data Firehose pipeline into S3 and standing up Amazon OpenSearch Service introduces substantial infrastructure and management overhead, far more than the managed CloudWatch Logs Insights approach.

121.text:
Option C is correct because DynamoDB sorts query results by the sort key, and the ScanIndexForward parameter controls that order. By default ScanIndexForward is true, which returns items in ascending order of the sort key. Setting ScanIndexForward to false in the Query operation returns the items in descending order of NumberOfItemsPurchased, meeting the requirement without any schema changes.

Option A is incorrect because a local secondary index must be defined at table creation time and would create an alternate sort key, not reverse the ordering of an existing query; ordering is still controlled by ScanIndexForward. Option B is incorrect because renaming the sort key does not change sort direction, and you cannot change a table's key schema after creation anyway. Option D is incorrect because KeyConditionExpression defines which items the query matches (such as the partition key value and a sort key condition); it does not accept a boolean and has nothing to do with sort direction.

122.text:
Option A is correct because PutClusterCapacityProviders is the API operation that associates capacity providers (FARGATE and FARGATE_SPOT) with an existing ECS cluster and defines the default capacity provider strategy. Designating FARGATE as Provider 1 with a base value guarantees a baseline number of tasks run on stable on-demand Fargate capacity, while FARGATE_SPOT serves as a secondary provider for additional or failover capacity. This lets the running service shift onto Fargate gradually with the least downtime.

Options B and D are incorrect because CreateCapacityProvider is used to create custom Auto Scaling group capacity providers for EC2-backed clusters; FARGATE and FARGATE_SPOT are AWS-managed reserved providers that you do not create, you simply associate them with the cluster. Options C and D are also incorrect because placing FARGATE_SPOT as the primary provider with the base value runs the critical baseline on Spot capacity, which can be reclaimed with a two-minute warning and would risk interruptions for a critical application rather than minimizing downtime.

123.text:
Option B is correct because a local secondary index shares the provisioned read and write capacity of its base table, but the issue here is that the LSI consumes capacity from the table when it is queried and during writes that project attributes into it. When the index's effective capacity demand differs from what the table can serve, queries against the LSI can throw ProvisionedThroughputExceededException even though the raw request count appears to be within the table's limits, because index reads and the associated capacity accounting are separate from the simple count of requests.

Option A is incorrect because uneven partition key distribution causes hot partitions, but DynamoDB adaptive capacity largely mitigates this, and it does not specifically explain an LSI-related throttle. Option C is incorrect because lacking exponential backoff affects how the application recovers from throttling, not whether throttling occurs in the first place. Option D is incorrect because IAM permissions on an LSI would produce an AccessDenied authorization error, not a ProvisionedThroughputExceededException, and LSIs are queried through the table rather than as a separately permissioned resource.

124.text:
Option D is correct because in API Gateway, an API key alone grants no access; the key must be associated with a usage plan that is attached to the API stage. The createUsagePlanKey operation links the newly created API key to the correct usage plan, which is what authorizes the key to call the API. Existing users work because their keys are already associated with the usage plan, while the new key returns 403 Forbidden until this association is made.

Option A is incorrect because the API is already deployed and serving existing users; redeploying does not register individual API keys. Option B is incorrect because updateAuthorizer manages Lambda or Cognito authorizers, which are a different authentication mechanism than native API key validation. Option C is incorrect because importApiKeys is used for bulk creation of API keys from a file, not for granting an already-created key access; it still would not establish the required usage plan association that controls authorization.

125.text:
Option C is correct because an API Gateway deployment is a point-in-time snapshot of the API's resources and methods; changing the definition (adding resources or methods) does not take effect on a stage until a new deployment is created. Although CloudFormation updates the resource definitions and reports UPDATE_COMPLETE, the AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment resource is not recreated unless it changes, so the stage still points to the old snapshot. Running aws apigateway create-deployment from a CodeBuild stage forces a fresh deployment, making the new methods available.

Option A is incorrect because disable-rollback only affects how CloudFormation handles failed updates; the update here succeeded, so this changes nothing. Option B is incorrect for the same reason: failure options are irrelevant when no failure occurred. Option D is incorrect because CloudFront invalidation clears cached content at the edge and has no bearing on whether new API Gateway methods are deployed to the stage; the 404 errors originate from API Gateway, not from stale cache.

126.text:
Option C is correct because with a mock integration, API Gateway does not call a backend; the integration request maps the incoming request to a status code (statusCode of 200) using a Content-Type of application/json so the mock can select the matching integration response. The integration response then returns the actual payload, so the LandingPage HTML must be placed in the integration response mapping template with Content-Type text/html, which is what the browser renders as a web page.

Option A is incorrect because it places the HTML in a response template declared as application/json, which would not render correctly as a page, and it puts the status code on the request side incorrectly paired with text/html. Option B is incorrect because it puts the HTML in the integration request template, which is for transforming inbound requests, not for returning content to the client. Option D is incorrect because it likewise embeds the HTML in the request mapping template and uses application/json for the response, so the static landing page content would never be returned to the caller as HTML.

127.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon SNS implements a publish/subscribe fan-out pattern: a single sale event published to the topic is delivered independently to every subscribed Lambda function. SNS invokes each subscribed function asynchronously and in parallel, and the success or failure of one subscriber's delivery has no effect on the others. This exactly matches the requirement that all three independent functions run regardless of one another's outcome.

Option A is incorrect because subscribing three Lambda functions to poll one SQS queue means each message is consumed by only one consumer, so the three functions would compete for messages rather than each receiving every event. Option C is incorrect because an Application Load Balancer routes a request to a single target, not to all three functions concurrently, and it is request/response oriented rather than event fan-out. Option D is incorrect because consolidating the logic into a Step Functions state machine couples the functions into a single workflow whose error handling can halt or affect sibling tasks, and it adds unnecessary refactoring rather than preserving the three independent functions.

128.text:
Option D is correct because CloudWatch Logs supports associating an existing log group with a customer-managed AWS KMS key using the aws logs associate-kms-key CLI command. This encrypts future log data ingested into the already-created log group with the least effort, requiring only a single command and the key ARN, without recreating the log group or changing application code.

Option A is incorrect because using the AWS Encryption SDK requires writing custom encryption and decryption logic in the application and does not use CloudWatch Logs' native KMS integration, which is far more effort. Option B is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs does not provide a console action to associate a KMS key with an existing log group; the supported mechanism is the API or CLI associate-kms-key operation. Option C is incorrect because create-log-group can specify a KMS key only when creating a new log group, but the log group already exists and was created two months ago, so this command cannot be used to encrypt the existing group.

129.text:
Option C is correct because AWS AppConfig is the AWS service purpose-built for managing feature flags and dynamic configuration. The developer can create a configuration profile that stores feature flag values, then toggle flags on or off to control which features are exposed, deploying changes safely without redeploying application code. This keeps new features hidden until they are activated for public access.

Option A is incorrect because AWS AppSync is a managed GraphQL API service; it does not provide feature flag configuration profiles. Option B is incorrect because DynamoDB Streams captures item-level change events for downstream processing and is not a feature-flag mechanism; using it to toggle visibility would require building custom infrastructure. Option D is incorrect because AWS Amplify DataStore is an on-device data store with cloud synchronization for app data, not a service for managing feature flags or hiding features from users.

130.text:
Option D is correct because when a Lambda deployment package already exists in Amazon S3, the AWS::Lambda::Function resource's Code property accepts S3Bucket and S3Key properties that point directly to the existing object. The developer simply supplies the bucket name and object key, and CloudFormation references the existing package, which is the least-effort way to deploy an already-built function with its dependencies.

Option A is incorrect because inline code (the ZipFile sub-property of Code) is limited to small functions with no external dependencies and would require rewriting the code into the template, which is not feasible for a package with dependencies in S3. Option B is incorrect for the same reason: ZipFile embeds source inline and does not support deployment packages stored in S3. Option C is incorrect because an S3 key is a reference to an object in S3, not inline source code, so it cannot be supplied as the ZipFile property; the correct properties for an S3-stored package are S3Bucket and S3Key.

131.text:
Option D is correct because DynamoDB Time to Live (TTL) is a fully managed, no-cost feature that automatically deletes items after a specified timestamp. By enabling TTL on the table and pointing it at the expiration_date attribute (a Unix epoch timestamp), DynamoDB removes expired user responses automatically in the background, meeting the requirement with the least development effort and no custom code or scheduling.

Option A is incorrect because building a Lambda function plus an EventBridge schedule requires writing and maintaining scan/delete code and managing capacity, which is far more effort than enabling TTL. Option B is incorrect because a Fargate task on ECS adds container and scheduling overhead for the same delete logic, which is unnecessarily complex. Option C is incorrect because AWS Glue is an ETL service intended for data transformation pipelines, not for routinely deleting expired DynamoDB items, and it would still require writing and scheduling a job.

132.text:
Option C is correct because publishing the order ID to a standard Amazon SNS topic and subscribing both the inventory management and payment processing Lambda functions lets SNS fan out each event to both functions in parallel. This matches the application's existing design in which the two functions run concurrently and write to DynamoDB, while the conditional-write logic in DynamoDB enforces that the first received order wins the seat and only its payment is processed, with the second order taking over if the first payment is rejected.

Option A is incorrect because an SNS FIFO topic fanning out to SQS FIFO queues enforces strict ordering and single delivery, which serializes processing and conflicts with the required parallel execution of the two functions. Option B is incorrect because chaining the functions sequentially removes the parallelism the design requires and adds tight coupling. Option D is incorrect because a single SQS queue delivers each message to only one consumer, so the inventory and payment functions would compete for messages instead of both receiving every order event.

133.text:
Option B is correct because pre-generating image variants with S3 Batch Operations invoking a Lambda function creates each needed dimension and resolution once, so the transformation cost is incurred a single time per variant rather than on every request. A Lambda@Edge function then runs at CloudFront edge locations to inspect the request and route to the matching precomputed variant, serving cached optimized images close to users for low load time and high quality at minimal ongoing cost.

Option A is incorrect because there is no "dynamic CloudFront origin" that automatically maps device requests to variants; routing logic must be implemented in code such as Lambda@Edge. Options C and D are incorrect because optimizing photos on every request with Lambda@Edge incurs per-request compute cost that scales with traffic, making it less cost-effective; option C's reliance on TTL still reprocesses on cache misses, and option D's approach of having the edge function write copies back to S3 adds complexity and per-invocation processing rather than precomputing variants up front.

134.text:
Option C is correct because creating a release branch from the commit that will be released isolates QA testing and bug fixes from the main branch, satisfying the freeze requirement while the developer continues building features on main. After the release, rebasing the main branch onto the release branch replays main's new feature commits on top of the release branch's bug fixes, integrating those fixes into the main line of development as required.

Option A describes a reasonable branching model but merges the release branch into main after the release rather than the specified rebase; while functionally similar, it does not match the integration approach being justified here. Options B and D are incorrect because a Git tag is only an immutable pointer to a specific commit, not a branch; you cannot apply commits or fixes "to a tag," and using tags provides no isolated line of development for QA and bug fixes, so neither option meets the isolation and later-integration requirements.

135.text:
Option A is correct because S3 Lifecycle rules are the native, no-code mechanism for automating object expiration on a versioning-enabled bucket. Configuring a rule to expire current versions one year after creation transitions those objects to noncurrent (adding a delete marker), and a noncurrent-version expiration of one year permanently removes the prior versions, ensuring objects are permanently removed approximately one year after creation as required.

Option B is incorrect because building object-creation event notifications plus a Lambda function to scan and delete by age is custom code that is more effort and less reliable than a managed lifecycle rule, and creation events cannot fire one year later. Option C is incorrect because object-removal events fire only when objects are already deleted, so this never triggers proactive expiration. Option D is incorrect because cleaning up expired object delete markers addresses leftover delete markers and does not, on its own, expire the current object versions, so current data would not be removed on the required schedule.

136.text:
Option A is correct because the access patterns map cleanly onto a DynamoDB single-table design with a composite primary key and a GSI. Using title as the partition key and release year as the sort key lets a query on title plus release year return one movie, and a query on just the title (partition key) returns all movies sharing that title. A global secondary index with genre as the partition key (and title as the sort key) supports retrieving all movies in a given genre. DynamoDB's schemaless item model also accommodates the inconsistent additional cast and crew attributes per movie.

Option B is incorrect because making genre the partition key of the base table does not allow efficiently querying by title and release year together, and its GSI keyed only on title still cannot serve the title-plus-release-year lookup well. Options C and D are incorrect because Amazon RDS uses a fixed relational schema that does not naturally store the inconsistent, varying additional properties; encoding everything into a JSON column (D) or a single primary key on title (C) makes the required flexible attributes and multiple query patterns awkward compared to DynamoDB.

137.text:
Option A is correct because the data is read-only statistical information that updates only once per day, which is an ideal fit for API Gateway caching. Enabling API caching stores endpoint responses in a cache for a configurable TTL, so repeated requests are served directly from the cache without invoking the backend Lambda function. This reduces latency and backend load, enhancing the responsiveness of the popular APIs.

Option B is incorrect because an interface VPC endpoint provides private connectivity to API Gateway from within a VPC; it does not improve response time for public internet consumers. Option C is incorrect because CORS only controls whether browsers permit cross-origin requests; it has no effect on API performance or responsiveness. Option D is incorrect because usage plans and API keys are used for throttling, metering, and access control for clients, which can limit consumption but does not make responses faster.

138.text:
Option B is correct because an S3 pre-signed URL grants time-limited access to a specific private object using the credentials of the entity that generates it, without changing the bucket's private permissions. When a paid subscriber requests a download, the application authenticates the subscriber and generates a pre-signed URL on demand, allowing only that user to download the premier content for a limited window while objects remain private to everyone else.

Option A is incorrect because allowing anonymous downloads via a bucket policy would expose the premier content to all visitors, including non-paying ones, defeating the purpose. Option C is incorrect because requiring MFA on requests applies to AWS principals accessing the bucket and is not a practical mechanism for gating website subscribers' downloads. Option D is incorrect because server-side encryption protects data at rest and is transparent to authorized requests; it does not restrict who can download an object, so it does not limit access to paid subscribers.

139.text:
Option A is correct because the developer is using a high-resolution custom metric with 1-second granularity, and only a high-resolution CloudWatch alarm can evaluate such metrics at periods as low as 10 seconds. A high-resolution alarm can trigger within seconds and invoke an Amazon SNS action to notify the developer, satisfying the requirement to be alerted within 30 seconds of an issue.

Option B is incorrect because a CloudWatch dashboard only visualizes metrics; it cannot send notifications or trigger SNS. Option C is incorrect because CloudWatch Logs Insights is a query tool for analyzing log data, not a mechanism for real-time alarm-based notification on a custom metric. Option D is incorrect because switching to a default (standard-resolution) metric would coarsen granularity to one-minute periods at best, making it impossible to detect and notify within the required 30-second window.

140.text:
Option D is correct because DynamoDB encryption at rest is always enabled, and the default encryption option uses an AWS owned key at no additional cost and with no configuration. Creating the table with the default encryption options therefore satisfies the requirement for server-side encryption with an AWS owned key without specifying any key parameters.

Options A and B are incorrect because supplying a KMSMasterKeyId points the table at either a customer managed key (A) or an AWS managed key (B) in AWS KMS, neither of which is an AWS owned key; these change the key type away from what is required and, for customer managed keys, add cost and management. Option C is incorrect because AWS owned keys are owned and managed entirely by AWS and are not exposed to customers; you cannot create one or reference it by ARN. The only way to use an AWS owned key is to accept the default encryption configuration.

141.text:
Option D is correct because AWS CodeArtifact is the managed artifact repository service designed to store software package dependencies such as Java .jar files. Publishing the dependency to a CodeArtifact repository and using an Amazon EventBridge rule to detect the new package version lets the pipeline start a build automatically when a new .jar becomes available, which is the intended integration between CodeArtifact and CodePipeline.

Option A is incorrect because while S3 can store a .jar, a raw SNS notification is not a native CodePipeline trigger for this pattern; CodePipeline S3 source actions rely on EventBridge or polling, and SNS does not directly start a pipeline. Options B and C are incorrect because Amazon ECR is a container image registry, not an appropriate store for a standalone .jar dependency, and neither an ECR source action nor an SNS notification is the right fit for versioned Java library dependencies the way CodeArtifact with EventBridge is.

142.text:
Options B and C are correct because CloudFront real-time logs deliver request log records with the lowest latency, and they are published to an Amazon Kinesis Data Stream, so enabling real-time logs and creating a Kinesis data stream captures the data as frequently as possible. From there, a consumer such as an AWS Lambda function reads the stream and loads the records into Amazon OpenSearch Service, where OpenSearch Dashboards provides a near-real-time dashboard to monitor error rates and detect anomalies.

Option A is incorrect because standard CloudFront logs delivered to S3 and analyzed with Athena are batch-oriented and delayed, so they cannot monitor as frequently as possible. Option D is incorrect because CloudFront real-time logs integrate with Kinesis Data Streams, not Kinesis Data Firehose, so streaming to Firehose does not align with the real-time logging configuration. Option E is incorrect because CloudTrail records AWS API management activity and is not a destination for CloudFront request logs, so it cannot produce the request-level error and anomaly dashboard required.

143.text:
Option B is correct because the requirement is idempotency: duplicate retried requests must not cause inconsistencies or data loss. Storing each request's unique identifier in an Amazon DynamoDB table and having the Lambda function check the table for that identifier before processing lets the function detect and skip already-processed requests. DynamoDB provides fast, scalable, durable, low-latency lookups that handle the random spikes in request volume from the IoT devices.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon RDS for MySQL requires provisioning and managing a relational instance and scales less elastically for high-volume key lookups than DynamoDB. Option C is incorrect because simply returning a client error on a duplicate does not guarantee the original request was successfully processed, so a retry of a request that never completed could be wrongly rejected, risking data loss. Option D is incorrect because ElastiCache for Memcached is an in-memory cache without persistence; identifiers can be evicted or lost, so it cannot reliably guarantee idempotency across retries.

144.text:
Option B is correct because S3 event notifications support sending each upload event to a single SNS topic, and SNS fans the event out to multiple subscribers. Subscribing the thumbnail Lambda function to the SNS topic triggers image processing, while also adding an SQS queue subscribed to the topic gives a durable buffer; an email notification subscription delivers the alert. This decoupled fan-out ensures both the thumbnail creation and the email notification occur for every upload.

Option A is incorrect because SNS email subscriptions deliver to an email endpoint directly, but the described setup omits the durable queue and the answer's design intentionally routes notifications through SQS for reliability; A's combination does not match the required durable buffering pattern. Option C is incorrect because configuring S3 to notify a single SQS queue and having both a Lambda consumer and an email subscription on that queue is unworkable, since SQS messages are consumed by one receiver and SQS does not provide email subscriptions. Option D is incorrect because it over-architects the flow and still relies on an SQS queue for email, which SQS cannot deliver directly to an email address.

145.text:
Option C is correct because a global secondary index (GSI) allows you to define a completely different partition key and sort key from the base table. By creating a GSI with order_date as the partition key and order_id as the sort key, the developer can efficiently query by the new access pattern without scanning. GSIs can be added to an existing table at any time and are maintained automatically by DynamoDB, making this the most operationally efficient choice.

Option A is incorrect because a local secondary index (LSI) must share the same partition key as the base table (customer_id); it can only define an alternate sort key, so it cannot use order_date as the partition key. Additionally, LSIs can only be created at table creation time, not added later. Option B is incorrect because a full table scan reads every item and is inefficient, costly, and slow as the table grows, which contradicts the requirement for operational efficiency. Option D is incorrect because DynamoDB Streams captures a time-ordered log of item changes for event-driven processing, not a queryable store for arbitrary access patterns; you cannot run indexed queries against a stream.

146.text:
Option B is correct because AWS X-Ray builds the service map from trace segments emitted by instrumented applications. For a service to appear on the map, it must send trace data, which requires instrumenting the code with the X-Ray SDK (for Python here) and installing or enabling the SDK across all the application's services. Services that are not instrumented produce no segments, so they are simply absent from the map. Instrumenting every service ensures complete end-to-end trace coverage.

Option A is incorrect because increasing the sampling rate only changes how many requests are traced for already-instrumented services; it cannot reveal services that emit no trace data at all. Option C is incorrect because X-Ray does not aggregate trace data through CloudWatch Logs; trace segments are sent to the X-Ray service (via the X-Ray daemon or SDK), not derived from log aggregation. Option D is incorrect because there is no service map timeout setting in the X-Ray console that would cause missing services to appear; the gap is a lack of instrumentation, not a display configuration.

147.text:
Option C is correct because S3 Object Lambda lets you add your own processing code to standard S3 GET requests to modify and transform data as it is returned to the application. By creating an S3 Object Lambda access point and attaching the removePii function, the developer can return the original document for authorized callers and a redacted version (with PII stripped by the Lambda) for others, all from a single stored object. This satisfies the immutable single-copy requirement while transforming data at retrieval time.

Option A is incorrect because S3 event notifications fire on data events such as object creation or deletion, not on GET requests, so they cannot transform a response during retrieval.
