A CloudWatch alarm set on that metric with a threshold of >=1 will trigger as soon as the error appears, and the alarm can publish to an Amazon SNS topic to notify the development team in near real time. This is a fully managed, no-code monitoring path with the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because AWS CloudTrail records API activity, not application log content, and there is no "CloudTrail alarm" for custom log keywords. Option B is incorrect because a scheduled Lambda function polling logs every 5 minutes adds custom code to maintain and is not real time. Option D is incorrect because the CloudWatch unified agent collects and ships logs and metrics but does not itself generate keyword-based metrics or send SNS notifications; that still requires a metric filter and alarm, making this more complex than option C.

296.text:
Option A is correct because a global secondary index lets you query a DynamoDB table using a completely different partition key and sort key than the base table. Creating a GSI with productFamily as the partition key and productType as the sort key enables efficient Query operations on those attributes without scanning the table. A GSI can be added to an existing table at any time and has its own provisioned/on-demand capacity, directly improving the performance of the frequent productFamily/productType lookups.

Option B is incorrect because a local secondary index must share the table's partition key (partNumber) and only provides an alternate sort key, so it cannot make productFamily the partition key. Option C is incorrect for the same reason and additionally requires recreating the table, which is unnecessary and disruptive since an LSI still cannot repartition on productFamily. Option D is incorrect because Scan reads the entire table and filters afterward, which is inefficient and costly compared with an indexed Query.

297.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed, serverless NoSQL database that delivers consistent single-digit-millisecond, low-latency reads and writes at any scale. Using a composite primary key of location ID (partition key) and timestamp (sort key) is an ideal time-series access pattern, letting customers query their current and historical usage efficiently. DynamoDB scales seamlessly with on-demand capacity without downtime or performance impact, making it the most cost-effective fit for unpredictable, growing high-volume meter ingestion.

Option A is incorrect because Amazon RDS requires instance scaling that can involve downtime and does not scale as seamlessly or cost-effectively for this high-write, high-cardinality workload. Option C is incorrect because ElastiCache for Redis is an in-memory store; retaining all historical readings in memory is expensive and risks data durability for a system of record. Option D is incorrect because S3 with Athena suits batch analytics, not low-latency on-demand per-customer lookups, and per-query Athena scans add latency unsuitable for interactive current-usage access.

298.text:
Option B is correct because Amazon RDS Proxy sits between the Lambda function and the RDS for MySQL instance and pools and shares database connections. During unpredictable peak traffic, many concurrent Lambda invocations would otherwise each open a database connection and exhaust the instance's max_connections, producing the "too many connections" errors. RDS Proxy reuses established connections, dramatically reducing the connection count, and it also improves failover resilience without requiring database downtime, directly meeting the resilience and availability requirements.

Option A is incorrect because decreasing vCPUs reduces capacity and merely raising max_connections strains memory without solving the connection-storm problem; both also require a reboot. Option C is incorrect because changing the DB instance class via an alarm causes a disruptive modification and possible downtime, violating the no-downtime requirement, and reacts slowly to spikes. Option D is incorrect because dynamically changing max_connections in response to CPU does not address connection exhaustion, adds operational complexity, and can require a restart.

299.text:
Option A is correct because Lambda environment variables are key-value pairs that you set on the function configuration outside the code. The function reads them at runtime using the standard mechanism for its language (for example, os.environ in Python). When the table name changes, you simply update the environment variable's value in the function configuration without modifying, repackaging, or redeploying the code, which is the most efficient way to externalize configuration.

Option B is incorrect because the /tmp directory is ephemeral scratch space that is not guaranteed to persist between invocations, and seeding a file there still requires deployment or runtime logic to populate it. Option C is incorrect because storing the name in a Lambda layer still requires republishing the layer and updating the function whenever the name changes, adding overhead. Option D is incorrect because a global variable outside the handler still hard-codes the value in the deployment package, so changing the table name would still require editing and redeploying the code.

300.text:
Option C is correct because the rolling with additional batch deployment policy first launches an extra batch of instances before taking any existing instances out of service, so capacity never drops below the desired count during deployment. With a batch size of 1, Elastic Beanstalk maintains full capacity (and briefly more), guaranteeing at least the five running instances stay available and never falling to four, while only paying for one additional instance for the duration of the deployment. This is the most cost-effective way to avoid the performance degradation.

Option A is incorrect because permanently raising the desired count to six adds ongoing cost even when no deployment is occurring. Option B is incorrect because traffic splitting performs a blue/green-style canary that launches a full parallel set of instances, which is more costly and complex than needed. Option D is incorrect because the rolling policy (without an additional batch) takes existing instances out of service in batches, reducing capacity below five during deployment and a batch size of 2 would drop it to three, worsening the problem.

301.text:
Option C is correct because storing each report in an Amazon S3 bucket with server-side encryption provides durable, scalable object storage that easily handles files larger than 1 MB. A presigned URL with an expiration date grants each customer time-limited access to their own unique report (set to expire after 8 hours), keeping the objects otherwise private. An S3 Lifecycle configuration rule automatically deletes objects older than 2 days, so cleanup is fully managed by S3 with no custom code, giving the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because DynamoDB items have a 400 KB limit, so reports larger than 1 MB cannot be stored as items. Option B is incorrect because SNS messages cannot carry large file attachments and email-based delivery does not satisfy on-demand access through the web app or the 8-hour access window cleanly. Option D is incorrect because an RDS database plus a scheduled Lambda function to purge expired records adds significant operational overhead compared with S3 Lifecycle rules and presigned URLs.

302.text:
Option A is correct because CloudFront evaluates cache behaviors by path pattern, and you can apply different viewer access settings per behavior. Adding a second cache behavior that points to the same S3 origin, matches the login page's path, and has unrestricted viewer access allows anyone to load the login page without a signed cookie or signed URL. The default cache behavior keeps restricted viewer access, so all private content still requires valid signed cookies, preserving security while fixing the 403 on the login page.

Option B is incorrect because it inverts the configuration so that everything except the login page would be the default behavior with the wrong restriction mapping, leaving private content exposed under the unrestricted default. Option C is incorrect because failover origins are for origin availability/redundancy, not for differentiating authenticated versus unauthenticated access, and path patterns are not set on origins. Option D is incorrect because adding a public bucket policy and bypassing CloudFront with a direct S3 URL undermines the OAI protection and risks exposing private content.

303.text:
Option B is correct because the performance problem is caused by storing user session state in the web server's memory, which does not work when scaling horizontally behind an Application Load Balancer. Moving session data to Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached externalizes the sessions into a fast, shared in-memory store so any instance can serve any user, freeing the web servers' memory and enabling clean scale-out. Keeping the order data in an Amazon RDS for MySQL DB instance provides a managed, durable relational store for the application data.

Option A is incorrect because hosting MySQL on an EC2 instance and storing sessions in the relational database adds management overhead and database load, and does not relieve the memory pressure efficiently. Option C is incorrect because Memcached is an in-memory cache unsuitable as the durable system of record for order details. Option D is incorrect because the EC2 instance store is ephemeral local disk tied to a single instance, so sessions would be lost on scale-in or instance failure and could not be shared across instances.

304.text:
Option A is correct because Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose has a built-in data transformation feature that invokes an AWS Lambda function on the records in the stream before delivery. The developer can implement the Lambda function to apply pattern-based logic that strips the personally identifiable customer identifiers, return the transformed records to Firehose, and set the Amazon S3 bucket as the delivery destination. This is the native, fully managed transformation path with no additional infrastructure to operate.

Option B is incorrect because Firehose cannot deliver to an arbitrary EC2 instance as a destination, and running a custom application on EC2 adds operational overhead. Option C is incorrect because OpenSearch Service is an analytics/search destination, not an in-line record transformation mechanism, and there is no built-in "search and replace" transform step in the delivery path. Option D is incorrect because Step Functions cannot be set as a Firehose delivery destination; Firehose supports inline Lambda transformation, not orchestrated workflows in the delivery stream.

305.text:
Option B is correct because a Lambda layer lets you package the shared Python library once and attach it to multiple functions. By creating a single layer containing the required library and referencing it from both Lambda functions, the library no longer needs to be bundled inside each function's deployment package, reducing each package's size below the quota. Layers are managed centrally, so updating the library in one place applies to both functions, which is the least operational overhead.

Option A is incorrect because packaging the library separately for each function still includes the full library in every deployment package, so it does not reduce the individual package sizes. Option C is incorrect because merging the two functions into one couples unrelated responsibilities, complicates the architecture, and may not even reduce the package below the quota. Option D is incorrect because Lambda cannot import a Python library by referencing an S3 object URL at runtime; dependencies must be available in the package or a layer, so this approach would not work.

306.text:
Option A is correct because every Lambda execution environment provides a writable /tmp directory (local ephemeral storage, at least 512 MB by default) intended exactly for scratch files created during an invocation. Files under 10 MB that are read and modified repeatedly during a single invocation and do not need to be persisted afterward fit perfectly in /tmp, which offers the fastest access with no additional services to configure or pay for.

Option B is incorrect because Amazon EFS is for durable, shared file storage across invocations and requires VPC and mount configuration, which is unnecessary overhead for throwaway temporary files. Option C is incorrect because Amazon EBS volumes attach to EC2 instances and cannot be mounted by Lambda functions at all. Option D is incorrect because Amazon S3 is durable object storage designed for persistence and remote access; using it for transient files that are modified many times in one invocation adds latency, cost, and complexity with no benefit.

307.text:
Option C is correct because AWS CodeCommit is a fully managed source control service that hosts secure, private Git repositories. It is the AWS service purpose-built for storing program source code, and it integrates directly with AWS CodePipeline as a source stage to trigger the CI/CD build and deployment workflow whenever code changes are pushed.

Option A is incorrect because AWS CodeDeploy automates the deployment of application code to compute targets such as EC2, Lambda, or ECS; it does not store source code. Option B is incorrect because AWS CodeArtifact is an artifact repository for software packages and dependencies (such as npm, Maven, or pip packages), not a source code repository. Option D is incorrect because Amazon CodeGuru provides automated code reviews and application performance profiling recommendations; it analyzes code but does not serve as the source repository for the pipeline.

308.text:
Options B and D are correct because they directly expose where time is being spent for an API Gateway request. Latency measures the total time from when API Gateway receives the request to when it returns a response to the client, while IntegrationLatency measures only the time between API Gateway forwarding the request to the backend (the Lambda integration) and receiving a response. Comparing these two metrics shows whether the delay is in the backend integration or in API Gateway's own processing, which is exactly what is needed when the API times out even though Lambda finishes within its limit.

Options A and C are incorrect because CacheHitCount and CacheMissCount only describe API Gateway response cache behavior and reveal nothing about request processing time or timeouts. Option E is incorrect because Count simply tallies the number of API requests in a period; it indicates traffic volume but provides no latency or timing information to diagnose the timeout.

309.text:
Option C is correct because AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store SecureString parameters store sensitive values encrypted with AWS KMS and can be retrieved securely by EC2 instances (via the SSM API/IAM role) and referenced directly within AWS CloudFormation stacks. For one-time fixed license keys that do not require automatic rotation, standard Parameter Store parameters are free, making this the most cost-effective secure option that satisfies both the EC2 automation scripts and the CloudFormation use case.

Option A is incorrect because storing encrypted files in S3 with a naming prefix is an ad hoc approach lacking centralized secret management and adds key-handling complexity. Option B is incorrect because AWS Secrets Manager charges per secret per month plus API calls and is designed for rotating credentials; for static license keys it is more expensive than Parameter Store, and storing the value in a tag named SecretString is a misuse. Option D is incorrect because CloudFormation NoEcho parameters only mask values in the console and do not provide durable, retrievable storage for EC2 scripts.

310.text:
Options A and B are correct because they protect the database from both accidental deletion and disruptive updates. Adding a DeletionPolicy attribute with the Retain value to the database resource tells CloudFormation to keep the RDS instance even if the resource is removed from the template or the stack is deleted, preventing data loss. A CloudFormation stack policy can explicitly deny update or replacement actions on the database resource during stack updates, which prevents the kind of drop-and-recreate replacement that caused the incident.

Option C is incorrect because a Multi-AZ deployment improves availability and failover but does nothing to prevent accidental deletion or replacement of the database. Option D is incorrect because stack sets manage deployments of stacks across multiple accounts and Regions and do not provide deletion protection for a specific resource. Option E is incorrect because DeletionPolicy is a resource-level attribute, not a stack-level one; applying it to the stack is invalid and would not protect the database resource.

311.text:
Option B is correct because configuring an S3 event notification to invoke an AWS Lambda function provides an event-driven, push-based pattern that fires the moment a new object is created in the bucket. The Lambda function receives the event with the object details and immediately runs code to insert the corresponding record into the Amazon DynamoDB table. This is the standard, low-latency, serverless integration for reacting to S3 object creation.

Option A is incorrect because, while EventBridge can receive S3 events, it cannot insert records into DynamoDB by itself; it still needs a target such as a Lambda function to perform the write, so the described step is incomplete. Option C is incorrect because polling the S3 bucket from a Lambda function is inefficient, introduces delay, and is not real time compared to event notifications. Option D is incorrect because a scheduled cron job runs on a fixed interval rather than reacting immediately to new files, so it cannot insert the record as soon as the file arrives.

312.text:
Option C is correct because the AWS Serverless Application Model (AWS SAM) is an open-source framework and extension of CloudFormation that provides simplified YAML shorthand syntax for defining serverless resources such as AWS Lambda functions, Amazon API Gateway APIs, and Amazon DynamoDB tables. SAM templates transform into standard CloudFormation, letting the developer concisely declare the entire serverless application in YAML, which is exactly the requirement.

Option A is incorrect because there is no such thing as "CloudFormation serverless intrinsic functions"; intrinsic functions like Ref and Fn::GetAtt are general-purpose template helpers, not a serverless authoring abstraction. Option B is incorrect because AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a platform for deploying and managing applications on provisioned infrastructure such as EC2, not a tool for defining serverless resources in templates. Option D is incorrect because the AWS CDK defines infrastructure using imperative programming languages (such as TypeScript or Python) rather than YAML, so it does not match the requirement to define resources in YAML.

313.text:
Option B is correct because Lambda versions are immutable snapshots of the function code and configuration, and an alias is a named pointer that can be shifted between published versions instantly. By repointing the alias from the current version to a previously published version, the developer can roll back quickly and seamlessly with no redeployment, no rebuilds, and minimal operational overhead. Callers invoke the alias rather than a specific version, so the change is transparent to clients and is a built-in native Lambda capability requiring no additional services.

Option A is incorrect because AWS OpsWorks is a configuration management service for Chef and Puppet aimed at EC2 and on-premises servers, not a mechanism for managing Lambda versions, and it adds unnecessary complexity. Option C is incorrect because manually storing and redeploying old packages from Amazon S3 reintroduces deployment steps and human error, which is the opposite of seamless and low overhead. Option D is incorrect because while AWS CodePipeline can orchestrate deployments and rollbacks, standing up and maintaining a pipeline is far heavier than simply switching an alias, so it does not meet the LEAST operational overhead requirement.
